blocks:
Ayahs

تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (١)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ فَٱعْبُدِ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصًا لَّهُ ٱلدِّينَ (٢)
Indeed We have sent down the Book to you with the truth; so worship Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him. (2)
أَلَا لِلَّهِ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْخَالِصُ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ مَا نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ زُلْفَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ فِى مَا هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَنْ هُوَ كَـٰذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ (٣)
Indeed, only exclusive faith is worthy of Allah, and those who take other as awliya besides Him [claiming,] ‘We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah,’ Allah will judge between them concerning that about which they differ. Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a liar and an ingrate. (3)
لَّوْ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ أَن يَتَّخِذَ وَلَدًا لَّٱصْطَفَىٰ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ (٤)
Had Allah intended to take a son, He could have chosen from those He has created whatever He wished. Immaculate is He! He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount. (4)
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ يُكَوِّرُ ٱلَّيْلَ عَلَى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُكَوِّرُ ٱلنَّهَارَ عَلَى ٱلَّيْلِ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ يَجْرِى لِأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۗ أَلَا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرُ (٥)
He created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom. He winds the night over the day, and winds the day over the night, and He has disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term. Indeed, He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiver! (5)
خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَزْوَٰجٍ ۚ يَخْلُقُكُمْ فِى بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ خَلْقًا مِّنۢ بَعْدِ خَلْقٍ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتٍ ثَلَـٰثٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُصْرَفُونَ (٦)
He created you from a single soul, then made from it its mate, and He has sent down for you eight mates of the cattle. He creates you in the wombs of your mothers, creation after creation, in a threefold darkness. That is Allah, your Lord! To Him belongs all sovereignty. There is no god except Him. Then where are you being led away? (6)
إِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ عَنكُمْ ۖ وَلَا يَرْضَىٰ لِعِبَادِهِ ٱلْكُفْرَ ۖ وَإِن تَشْكُرُوا۟ يَرْضَهُ لَكُمْ ۗ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُم مَّرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٧)
If you are ungrateful, Allah has indeed no need of you, though He does not approve ingratitude for His servants; and if you give thanks, He approves that for you. No bearer shall bear another’s burden; then your return will be to your Lord, whereat He will inform you concerning what you used to do. Indeed, He knows best what is in the breasts. (7)
۞ وَإِذَا مَسَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ ضُرٌّ دَعَا رَبَّهُۥ مُنِيبًا إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ إِذَا خَوَّلَهُۥ نِعْمَةً مِّنْهُ نَسِىَ مَا كَانَ يَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ مِن قَبْلُ وَجَعَلَ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا لِّيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۚ قُلْ تَمَتَّعْ بِكُفْرِكَ قَلِيلًا ۖ إِنَّكَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ (٨)
When distress befalls man, he supplicates his Lord, turning to Him penitently. Then, when He grants him a blessing from Himself, he forgets that for which he had supplicated Him earlier and sets up equals to Allah, that he may lead [people] astray from His way. Say, ‘Revel in your ingratitude for a while. Indeed you are among the inmates of the Fire.’ (8)
أَمَّنْ هُوَ قَـٰنِتٌ ءَانَآءَ ٱلَّيْلِ سَاجِدًا وَقَآئِمًا يَحْذَرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَيَرْجُوا۟ رَحْمَةَ رَبِّهِۦ ۗ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ۗ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٩)
Is he who supplicates in the watches of the night, prostrating and standing, apprehensive of the Hereafter and expecting the mercy of his Lord...? Say, ‘Are those who know equal to those who do not know?’ Only those who possess intellect take admonition. (9)
قُلْ يَـٰعِبَادِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةٌ ۗ وَأَرْضُ ٱللَّهِ وَٰسِعَةٌ ۗ إِنَّمَا يُوَفَّى ٱلصَّـٰبِرُونَ أَجْرَهُم بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (١٠)
Say, ‘[Allah declares:] “O My servants who have faith! Be wary of your Lord. For those who do good in this world there will be a good [reward], and Allah’s earth is vast. Indeed the patient will be paid in full their reward without any reckoning.” ’ (10)

(Commentary)

It appears from the verses of this Surah that the polytheists (المشركون) among the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asked him to desist from the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the call toward it, and to stop opposing their gods. They attempted to intimidate him with their gods, so this Surah was revealed—which is, in a sense, a counterpart to Surah Sad. It emphasizes the command to devote his religion (الدين) — the way of life and submission — sincerely to Allah, the Glorified, to pay no heed to their gods, and to inform them that he is commanded toward monotheism and the sincerity of religion (إخلاص الدين), a matter upon which verses have consecutively converged through both revelation (الوحي) and reason (العقل).

Therefore, we see the Glorified repeatedly returning the discourse to this theme throughout the Surah, such as His saying at the opening of the Surah: فاعبد الله مخلصا له الدين ألا لله الدين الخالص "So worship Allah, making religion pure for Him. Look! [Only] exclusive religion belongs to Allah" (39:2-3). Then He returns to it, saying:

قل إني أمرت أن أعبد الله مخلصا له الدين — until His saying — قل الله أعبد مخلصا له ديني فاعبدوا ما شئتم من دونه "Say, ‘I have been commanded to worship Allah, making religion pure for Him...’ Say, ‘Allah [alone] do I worship, making my religion pure for Him. So worship whatever you wish besides Him’" (39:11-15).

Then He says: إنك ميت وإنهم ميتون "You will surely die, and they [too] will surely die" (39:30), etc. Then He says: أليس الله بكاف عبده ويخوفونك بالذين من دونه "Does not Allah suffice His servant? They would frighten you with those [they worship] besides Him" (39:36). Then He says: قل يا قوم اعملوا على مكانتكم إني عامل "Say, ‘O my people! Act according to your ability; I too am acting’" (39:39). Then He says: قل أفغير الله تأمروني أعبد أيها الجاهلون "Say, ‘Do you bid me worship other than Allah, O ignorant ones?’" (39:64), among other such indications.

Furthermore, He generalized the argumentation for His uniqueness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية) — the exclusive right of Allah to create, sustain, and govern the universe — and Divinity (الألوهية) — the exclusive right of Allah to be worshipped — through both revelation and the path of demonstration (البرهان). He drew subtle comparisons between the believers (المؤمنين) and the polytheists, describing the believers with the most beautiful attributes and giving them glad tidings of their future reward in the Hereafter (الآخرة) time and again. He mentioned the polytheists, warning them of the loss and the punishment of the Hereafter that will befall them, in addition to the evil consequences of their affairs that strike them in this world, just as the punishment of ignominy in the life of this world struck the past nations who disbelieved—and the punishment of the Hereafter is greater.

Consequently, the Surah describes the Day of Resurrection (يوم البعث), especially in its conclusion, with the clearest and most complete description.

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses, and it seems to have been revealed all at once due to the interconnectedness between its verses.

The ten verses under discussion consolidate the call through the path of revelation and rational proof (الحجة العقلية), beginning with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying, the Exalted: تنزيل الكتاب من الله العزيز الحكيم "The sent-down [revelation] of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise" (39:1). "The sent-down [revelation] of the Book" (تنزيل الكتاب) is the predicate (خبر) of a suppressed subject (مبتدأ). It is an infinitive (مصدر) used in the sense of the object (المفعول); thus, its annexation to "the Book" is the annexation of an attribute to its described object. "From Allah" (من الله) is related to "sent-down," and the meaning is: "This is a Book sent down from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise."

It has also been said that "The sent-down [revelation] of the Book" is the subject and "from Allah" is its predicate, but perhaps the first view is more intuitive.

His saying, the Exalted: إنا أنزلنا إليك الكتاب بالحق فاعبد الله مخلصا له الدين "Indeed We have sent down the Book to you with the truth; so worship Allah, making religion pure for Him" (39:2). He used the form inzal (الإنزال) — sending down as a whole — rather than tanzil (التنزيل) — gradual revelation — as in the previous verse, because the intent is to state that it is "with the truth," which suits the totality of what was sent down to him from his Lord.

His saying: "with the truth" (بالحق), the preposition ba (ب) denotes accompaniment (الملابسة); meaning, We sent it down to you accompanied by the truth. Thus, the command therein to worship Allah alone is truth. Based on this meaning, He branched from it His saying: "so worship Allah, making religion pure for Him" (فاعبد الله مخلصا له الدين). The meaning is: since it is with the truth, then worship Allah, making religion pure for Him, because the Book contains that.

What is meant by religion (الدين)—according to what the context provides—is worship (العبادة). It is also possible that it refers to the "norm of life" (سنة الحياة), which is the path trodden in life within human society. In this case, worship would mean the manifestation of servitude (العبودية) by following the path legislated by Allah, the Glorified. The meaning would be: manifest servitude to Allah in all affairs of your life by following what He has legislated for you therein, while you are sincere to Him in your religion, not following anything other than what He has legislated for you.

His saying, the Exalted: ألا لله الدين الخالص "Look! [Only] exclusive religion belongs to Allah" (39:3). This is a manifestation and declaration of what was implied and summarized in His saying "with the truth," and a generalization of what was specified in His saying "so worship Allah, making religion pure for Him." That is, the sincerity of religion to Allah that We revealed to you is obligatory upon everyone who hears this call. Because the sentence is an independent proclamation, the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة) was made explicit, whereas the apparent requirement of the context would have been to use a pronoun and say: "to Him belongs the exclusive religion."

The meaning of the exclusive religion belonging to Him is that He does not accept worship from one who does not worship Him alone, whether they worship Him along with another or worship another exclusively.

His saying, the Exalted: والذين اتخذوا من دونه أولياء ما نعبدهم إلا ليقربونا إلى الله زلفى "As for those who take guardians besides Him, [saying,] ‘We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah’" (39:3) until the end of the verse. It was previously mentioned that the pagans (الوثنية) believe Allah, the Glorified, is too sublime for human perception—whether through intellect, imagination, or sense—to encompass Him. Thus, He is transcendent above being the direct object of our acts of worship.

Therefore, they believe it is necessary to seek nearness to Him by seeking nearness to the honored ones among His creation, those to whom the management of the world's affairs has been delegated. Thus, they take them as lords (أربابا) besides Allah, then as gods (آلهة) whom they worship and seek nearness to, so that they may intercede (يشفعوا) for them with Allah and bring them closer to Him in station. These are the angels, the jinn, and the holy ones among humans; these are the true lords and gods in their view.

As for the manufactured idols (الأصنام) placed in shrines and temples, they are merely images (تماثيل) of those lords and gods; they are not lords or gods in themselves. However, the ignorant among their masses might not distinguish between the idols and the lords of the idols, so they worship the idols just as the lords and gods are worshipped. Such was the case with the Arabs of the Jahiliyyah, and similarly, the ignorant among the Sabians might not have distinguished between the idols of the stars and the stars themselves—which are also idols for the spirits (أرواح) entrusted over them—and those spirits which are the true lords and gods according to their elite.

In any case, the lords and gods are the ones worshipped by them; they are contingent beings (موجودات ممكنة) created by Allah, brought near to Him, and delegated with the management of the world's affairs, each according to their rank. As for Allah, the Glorified, He possesses nothing but creation and origination; He is the Lord of lords and the God of gods.

If you recall what has passed, it becomes clear that the meaning of His saying: "As for those who take guardians (أولياء) besides Him" refers to their taking them as lords who manage affairs, by attributing lordship and the matter of management to them rather than to Allah. Thus, they are the managers of affairs in their view. From this follows the branching of submission and worship to them, because worship is for seeking benefit, repelling harm, or thanking for blessings—and all of that is attributed to them due to their undertaking the matter of management instead of Allah, the Glorified.

Thus, the meaning of taking them as "guardians" is taking them as lords (١). For this reason, the taking of guardians was followed by the mention of worship: "We only worship them so that they may bring us near." So, His saying: "As for those who take guardians besides Him" is the subject (مبتدأ), and its predicate (خبر) is "Indeed Allah will judge..." etc. They refer to the polytheists who believe in the lordship and divinity of partners besides Allah, except for what their ignorant ones believed regarding Him, the Exalted, being a partner with them in being worshipped.

His saying: "We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah in station" (ما نعبدهم إلا ليقربونا إلى الله زلفى) is an explanation of the meaning of taking guardians besides Allah. It is a narration of their speech, or with a suppressed verb of "saying"; i.e., they say: "We do not worship these except so that they may bring us near to Allah by virtue of our worship of them." Thus, they are turning away from Him, the Exalted, to others. They were only called polytheists (مشركين) because they associate others with Him, the Exalted, by claiming they are lords and gods of the world, and that He, the Exalted, is the Lord and God of those lords and gods. As for partnership in creation and origination (الخلق والإيجاد), neither polytheist nor monotheist has ever claimed it.

His saying: إن الله يحكم بينهم في ما هم فيه يختلفون "Indeed Allah will judge between them concerning that about which they differ" (39:3). It is said that the plural pronoun refers to the polytheists and their guardians; i.e., Allah will judge between the polytheists and their guardians regarding that which they differ. It is also said that both pronouns refer to the polytheists and their opponents among the people of sincerity in religion (أهل الإخلاص) understood from the context; the meaning being that Allah will judge between them and those sincere in religion.

His saying: إن الله لا يهدي من هو كاذب كفار "Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a liar and an ingrate" (39:3). The "ingrate" (الكفار) is one who is excessive in ingratitude for Allah's blessings or excessive in concealing the truth. In the sentence, there is an indication, or rather a proof, that the judgment on the Day of Resurrection will be against the polytheists, not for them, and that they are being driven toward punishment. Guidance (الهداية) here means leading to a good end.

His saying, the Exalted: لو أراد الله أن يتخذ ولدا لاصطفى مما يخلق ما يشاء سبحانه هو الله الواحد القهار "Had Allah intended to take a son, He would have chosen from what He creates whatever He wished. Immaculate is He! He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (39:4). This is an argument refuting their claim that Allah has taken a son, and the claim of some of them that the angels are the daughters of Allah. The claim of having offspring is prevalent among various pagan sects; the Christians said: "The Messiah is the son of God," and the Jews said—as the Quran narrates from them: "Ezra is the son of God," though it was perhaps a sonship of honor.

Sonship (البنوة), in whatever form, necessitates some form of partnership between the son and the father, the offspring and the parent. If it is a literal sonship—which is the derivation of one thing from another and its separation from it—it necessitates partnership in the reality of the essence (الذات), its properties, and the effects emanating from the essence, like the sonship of a human to a human, which necessitates the son's partnership with his father in humanity and its requirements. If it is a conventional sonship, such as social sonship (adoption), it necessitates partnership in the specific statuses of the father, such as leadership, kingdom, honor, precedence, inheritance, and some rulings of lineage. The argument presented in the verse demonstrates the impossibility of Him, the Exalted, taking a son in both senses.

His saying: "Had Allah intended to take a son" (لو أراد الله أن يتخذ ولدا) is a conditional clause beginning with law (لو), which indicates the impossibility of the consequent due to the impossibility of the antecedent. His saying: "He would have chosen from what He creates whatever He wished" (لاصطفى مما يخلق ما يشاء) means He would have selected for that purpose from what He creates whatever His will attached to, as provided by the context; and its being "from what He creates" is because everything other than Him, the Glorified, is His creation.

His saying: "Immaculate is He!" (سبحانه) is a sanctification of Him, the Glorified. His saying: "He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (هو الله الواحد القهار) is an explanation of the impossibility of the condition—which is the intention to take a son—so that the impossibility of the result follows, which is the choosing of what He wishes from what He creates. This is because He, the Glorified, is One (واحد) in His transcendent essence; nothing shares it with Him, and no one resembles Him in it, according to the proofs of monotheism. He is One in His essential attributes (الصفات الذاتية) which are identical to His essence, such as Life, Knowledge, and Power; and He is One in His affairs which are requirements of His essence, such as Creation, Sovereignty, Might, and Grandeur—no one shares these with Him.

He, the Glorified, is the All-paramount (القهار) — the One who prevails over all things by His essence and attributes. Nothing stands independent against His essence and existence in its own essence and existence, nor is anything independent of Him in its attributes and the effects of its existence. All are lowly and abased in relation to Him, owned by Him, and in need of Him.

The summary of the verse's argument is a simple hypothetical syllogism (قياس استثنائي) where the negation of the antecedent is used to conclude the negation of the consequent. It is like our saying: "If Allah had intended to take a son, He would have chosen for that some of what He wishes from His creation; but His intention to take a son is impossible because He is One and All-paramount; therefore, His choosing some of what He wishes from His creation for that purpose is impossible."

Some have offered a strange interpretation of the verse's argument, saying: "The result of the meaning is: had He, the Glorified, intended to take a son, that intention would have been impossible because it attaches to the impossible—namely, the taking [of a son]. But it is not permissible for the Creator to have an impossible intention because it would prefer some possibilities over others. The root of the discourse is: 'If He had taken a son, it would be impossible because it entails what contradicts Divinity.' So it was shifted to 'If He had intended to take a son, it would be impossible for Him to intend it' to be more eloquent. Then this answer was deleted and replaced with 'He would have chosen' to point out that this [choosing] is what is possible, not the former, and that if this had anything to do with taking a son, it would have been permissible for Him—exalted is He above that. Thus the entailment is established, and the negation of the consequent and the affirmation of the antecedent's impossibility are achieved without difficulty." End quote.

It seems this was taken from al-Zamakhshari's words in al-Kashshaf in the commentary on the verse, where he said: "Meaning, if He had intended to take a son, it would have been impossible and incorrect because it is absurd. It would not have been possible except that He chooses some of His creation and singles them out and brings them near, just as a man singles out his son and brings him near. He has already done that with the angels, yet you were tempted by it and His singling them out deceived you, so you claimed they were His children out of your ignorance of Him and His reality, which differs from the realities of bodies and accidents. It is as if He said: 'If He had intended to take a son, He would not have done more than what He did of choosing what He wishes from His creation, namely the angels. But you, due to your ignorance of Him, thought their being chosen was their being taken as children. Then you persisted in your ignorance and folly and made them daughters, so you were liars and ingrates, excessive in fabrication against Allah and His angels, and extreme in disbelief.'" End quote.

You are well aware that the context of the verse does not suit this explanation. Furthermore, it does not refute the claim of one who believes in a "legislated adoption" (تبني تشريفي), such as the Jews' saying "Ezra is the son of God," for they do not mean by adoption anything other than the choosing of whom He wishes from His creation.

There are other interpretations from them that are of no use, which we have omitted.

His saying, the Exalted: خلق السماوات والأرض بالحق "He created the heavens and the earth with the truth" (39:5). It is not unlikely that the reference to creation and management therein is an explanation of His being the All-paramount. However, the connection between the two verses and their thematic link, and the ending of the second with His saying: "That is Allah, your Lord..." etc., is almost explicit that this is a new beginning of the discourse to argue for the oneness of Lordship.

Thus, this verse and the one following it are directed toward the monotheism of Lordship. He has combined creation and management (التدبير) therein because, as mentioned repeatedly, proving the oneness of the Creator does not necessitate, in the view of the pagan, the negation of the plurality of lords and gods. They do not deny that creation and origination are restricted to Him, the Exalted. However, He, the Glorified, in His discourse arguing for His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity, combines creation and management to indicate that management is not outside of creation; rather, it is creation in one sense, just as creation is management in another. At that point, the argument is completed: that management returns to Him, the Exalted, and is restricted to Him, just as creation returns to Him.

His saying: "He created the heavens and the earth with the truth" refers to the creation. In His saying: "with the truth" (بالحق)—where the preposition denotes accompaniment—there is an indication of the Resurrection (البعث). For the creation being "truth" and not "vain" necessitates its being for a goal it intends and toward which it is driven, which is the Resurrection. He, the Exalted, said: وما خلقنا السماء والأرض وما بينهما باطلا "We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain" (38:27).

His saying: يكور الليل على النهار ويكور النهار على الليل "He winds the night over the day, and winds the day over the night" (39:5). It is said in al-Majma': "Winding" (التكوير) is to cast one thing upon another. End quote. So the meaning is casting the night upon the day and casting the day upon the night. This would be a metaphorical expression (استعارة بالكناية) close in meaning to His saying: يغشي الليل النهار "He draws the night's cover over the day" (7:54). The intent is the continuous succession of night and day by the appearance of this over that, then that over this, and so on; this is part of the management.

His saying: وسخر الشمس والقمر كل يجري لأجل مسمى "and He has disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term" (39:5). That is, He disposed the sun and the moon and made them run for the system current in the terrestrial world until a specified, determined term that they do not exceed.

His saying: ألا هو العزيز الغفار "Look! He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiver" (39:5). It is possible that the mention of these two names is an indication of what is argued for His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity. For the All-mighty (العزيز) — the One who is never touched by lowliness — if such exists, it is Allah, and He is the one designated for worship, not any other who is covered by lowliness and overwhelmed by need. Likewise, the All-forgiver (الغفار) of sins, when compared to one whose nature is not such.

It is also possible that their mention is an incitement toward monotheism and faith in the One Allah. The meaning would be: "I alert you that He is the All-mighty, so believe in Him and be empowered by His might; the All-forgiver, so believe in Him and He will forgive you."

His saying, the Exalted: خلقكم من نفس واحدة ثم جعل منها زوجها "He created you from a single soul, then made from it its mate" (39:6) etc. The address is to all of humanity. What is meant by the "single soul" (النفس الواحدة)—as supported by similar verses—is Adam, the father of humanity. What is meant by "its mate" (زوجها) is his wife, who is of his kind and resembles him in humanity. "Then" (ثم) denotes a sequence in the order of the discourse.

The meaning is that He, the Exalted, created this species and multiplied its individuals from a single soul and its mate.

His saying: وأنزل لكم من الأنعام ثمانية أزواج "and He has sent down for you eight mates of the livestock" (39:6). The livestock (الأنعام) are camels, cattle, sheep, and goats. Their being "eight mates" is in consideration of their division into male and female.

The naming of the creation of livestock on earth as "sending down" (إنزالا) is because He, the Exalted, calls the appearance of things in existence after they were not as "sending them down" from His treasuries which are with Him, and from the Unseen (الغيب) to the Manifest (الشهادة). He, the Exalted, said: وإن من شيء إلا عندنا خزائنه وما ننزله إلا بقدر معلوم "There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21).

His saying: يخلقكم في بطون أمهاتكم خلقا من بعد خلق في ظلمات ثلاث "He creates you in your mothers’ bellies, creation after creation, in a threefold darkness" (39:6). This is an explanation of the manner of creating the aforementioned humans and livestock. In the address, those possessed of intellect are given precedence over others. "Creation after creation" refers to the succession and sequence, such as the creation of the drop (النطفة) into a clinging mass (علقة), and the creation of the clinging mass into a fleshy lump (مضغة), and so on. The "threefold darkness" (الظلمات الثلاث) is the darkness of the belly, the womb, and the placenta, as has been said and narrated in al-Majma' from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

It has been said that it refers to the darkness of the loins, the womb, and the placenta, but this is an error, for His saying: "in your mothers' bellies" is explicit that the threefold darkness refers to what is in the bellies of women, not the loins of men.

His saying: ذلكم الله ربكم "That is Allah, your Lord" (39:6). That is, the One who has been described to you in the two verses by creation and management is your Lord, not any other. For the Lord (الرب) is the Owner who manages the affairs of what He owns. Since He is your Creator and the Creator of everything besides you, and the Creator of the system current within you, He is the One who owns you and manages your affairs; thus, He is your Lord and no other.

His saying: له الملك "to Him belongs all sovereignty" (39:6). That is, over all creatures in this world and the Hereafter; He is the Sovereign (المليك) in the absolute sense. The precedence of the prepositional phrase denotes restriction (الحصر). The sentence is a predicate after a predicate for His saying: "That is Allah," just as His saying: لا إله إلا هو "there is no god except Him" (39:6) is likewise. The restriction of Divinity to Him, the Exalted, is a branch of the restriction of Lordship to Him, because the God (الإله) is only worshipped because He is a managing Lord; thus He is worshipped either out of fear of Him, hope in Him, or gratitude to Him.

His saying: فأنى تصرفون "So where are you being led away?" (39:6). That is, how are you turned away from His worship to the worship of others, when He is your Lord who created you and managed your affair, and He is the Sovereign over you?

His saying, the Exalted: إن تكفروا فإن الله غني عنكم ولا يرضى لعباده الكفر "If you are ungrateful, Allah is indeed independent of you, and He does not approve ungratefulness for His servants" (39:7) until the end of the verse. This is directed toward explaining that the call to monotheism and the sincerity of religion to Allah, the Glorified, is not due to any need on His part for them to turn to Him by turning away from the worship of others. Rather, it is out of His care for them; thus He calls them to their happiness out of concern for it, just as He cares for their provision and pours blessings upon them, and just as He cares for their protection and inspires them to repel harms from themselves.

So His saying: "If you are ungrateful, Allah is indeed independent of you" (إن تكفروا فإن الله غني عنكم). The address is to all those legally responsible (المكلفين); i.e., if you disbelieve in Allah and do not unify Him, He is independent of you by His essence. He does not benefit from your faith and obedience, nor is He harmed by your disbelief and disobedience. Benefit and harm are only realized in the realm of possibility and need; as for the Necessary Being (الواجب) who is independent by His essence, neither benefit nor harm is conceivable in His regard.

His saying: "and He does not approve ungratefulness for His servants" (ولا يرضى لعباده الكفر) is a rebuttal to what might be imagined from His saying: "Allah is indeed independent of you"—that if He is not harmed by disbelief nor benefited by faith, then there is no reason for Him to want faith and gratitude from us. So He refuted this by stating that the attachment of Divine care to you necessitates that He does not approve of your disbelief, while you are His servants.

What is meant by "disbelief" (الكفر) here is the ingratitude for blessings (كفر النعمة), which is the abandonment of gratitude, as evidenced by the contrast with His saying: "and if you are grateful, He will approve it for you" (وإن تشكروا يرضه لكم). From this, it becomes clear that the expression "for His servants" (لعباده), rather than saying "for you," is to indicate the cause of the ruling—namely, the reason for the lack of approval.

The summary is that you are servants owned by Allah, the Glorified, immersed in His blessings. The bond of mastership and servitude (المولوية والعبودية)—which is the relationship of ownership and being owned—does not suit the servant being ungrateful for the blessings of his master, forgetting the guardianship (ولاية) of his Master, taking guardians for himself besides Him, and disobeying the Master while obeying His enemy, while he is a servant upon whom the mark of servitude is placed, possessing for himself neither benefit nor harm.

His saying: "and if you are grateful, He will approve it for you" (وإن تشكروا يرضه لكم). The pronoun refers to gratitude (الشكر), similar to His saying, the Exalted: اعدلوا هو أقرب للتقوى "Be just; that is nearer to God-consciousness" (5:8). The meaning is: if you thank Allah by acting according to the requirements of servitude and the sincerity of religion to Him, He will approve that gratitude for you, while you are His servants. Gratitude and the disbelief (ingratitude) that opposes it correspond to faith and the disbelief that opposes it.

From what has passed, it appears that "the servants" in His saying: "and He does not approve ungratefulness for His servants" is general, including everyone. Thus, the claim of some that it is specific, referring to those He meant in His saying: إن عبادي ليس لك عليهم سلطان إلا من اتبعك من الغاوين "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (15:42)—who are the sincere ones, or the infallible ones (المعصومون) according to al-Zamakhshari's interpretation—and its implication that Allah, the Glorified, approved faith for those who believed and approved disbelief for those who disbelieved except the infallible ones, for whom He intended faith and protected them from disbelief, is very weak. The context completely rejects it, as the discourse would then imply His approval of disbelief for the disbeliever. The meaning of the discourse would then boil down to something like: "If you disbelieve, Allah is independent of you, and He does not approve disbelief for the prophets, for example, because He approves faith for them; and if you are grateful, He approves it for you, and if you disbelieve, He approves it for you." This—as you see—is a poor and degraded meaning, especially given its occurrence in the context of the call.

Furthermore, the prophets, for example, are included among those who were grateful, and He has already approved gratitude and faith for them and did not approve disbelief for them; thus, there is no reason to single them out for mention when the approval for those who are grateful has already been mentioned.

His saying: ولا تزر وازرة وزر أخرى "No bearer shall bear another’s burden" (39:7). That is, no soul carrying a burden shall carry the burden of another soul; i.e., no one is taken to account for a sin except the one who committed it.

His saying: ثم إلى ربكم مرجعكم فينبئكم بما كنتم تعملون إنه عليم بذات الصدور "Then to your Lord will be your return, whereat He will inform you of what you used to do. Indeed, He knows best what is in the breasts" (39:7). That is, this [disbelief or gratitude] is in this world, then Allah will resurrect you and manifest to you the reality of your deeds and hold you to account for what is in your hearts. The discussion on the meanings of these phrases has been repeated previously.

(A Discourse on the Meaning of Approval and Displeasure from Allah)

Approval (الرضا) is among the meanings attributed to those possessed of consciousness and will, and its opposite is displeasure (السخط); both are existential attributes.

Furthermore, approval attaches to meanings such as attributes and actions, rather than essences (الذات). It is said: "He was pleased with such for him" or "He was pleased with such." The Exalted said: ولو أنهم رضوا ما آتاهم الله ورسوله "If only they had been pleased with what Allah and His Apostle had given them" (9:59), and He said: ورضوا بالحياة الدنيا "and are pleased with the life of this world" (10:7). What might attach to essences is only by some consideration and ultimately returns to a meaning, such as His saying: ولن ترضى عنك اليهود ولا النصارى "Never will the Jews be pleased with you, nor the Christians" (2:120).

Approval is not identical to will (الإرادة), even if everything to which the will attaches becomes an object of approval after its occurrence in some way. This is because the will—as has been said—attaches to a matter that has not occurred, while approval only attaches to a matter after its occurrence or the assumption of its occurrence. Therefore, a person being "pleased" with such an action means they are such that the action is compatible with them and not repulsive to them; it is an attribute subsisting in the one who approves, not the one approved.

Then, because approval attaches to a matter after its occurrence, it is realized by the realization of the approved object, becoming originated (حادثا) with its origination. Thus, it is impossible for it to be one of the attributes subsisting in His essence, due to His transcendence, the Exalted, above being a locus for originated events. So, what is attributed to Him, the Exalted, of approval is an attribute of action (صفة فعل) subsisting in His action, abstracted from it, like mercy, anger, will, and dislike. The Exalted said: رضي الله عنهم ورضوا عنه "Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him" (98:8), and He said: وأن أعمل صالحا ترضاه "and that I may do righteous deeds that please You" (27:19), and He said: ورضيت لكم الإسلام دينا "and I have approved Islam as your religion" (5:3).

His approval, the Exalted, of a matter is the compatibility of His action, the Exalted, with it. Since His action is of two types, ontological (تكويني) and legislative (تشريعي), approval from Him is also divided into ontological and legislative. Every ontological matter—which is what Allah willed and brought into existence—is approved by Him with an ontological approval, in the sense that His action, which is its origination from His volition (مشية), is compatible with what He originated. Every legislative matter—which is what the legal obligation (التكليف) of belief or action attaches to, such as faith and righteous deeds—is approved by Him with a legislative approval, in the sense of the compatibility of His legislation with what has been performed.

As for what opposes these commanded matters, to which a prohibition attaches, approval never attaches to them due to the lack of compatibility of the legislation with them, such as disbelief and transgression. As the Exalted said: إن تكفروا فإن الله غني عنكم ولا يرضى لعباده الكفر "If you are ungrateful, Allah is indeed independent of you, and He does not approve ungratefulness for His servants" (39:7), and He said: فإن ترضوا عنهم فإن الله لا يرضى عن القوم الفاسقين "Even if you are pleased with them, Allah is not pleased with the transgressing lot" (9:96).

His saying, the Exalted: وإذا مس الإنسان ضر دعا ربه منيبا إليه "When distress befalls man, he supplicates his Lord, turning to Him penitently" (39:8) until the end of the verse. "Penitence" (الإنابة) is returning, and "bestowal" (التخويل) is a great gift given as a grant or a boon, as stated in al-Majma'.

Since the previous verse mentioned those who are ungrateful for blessings and that Allah, the Glorified, despite His independence from people, does not approve that for them, He pointed out in this verse that man is ungrateful by nature, even though he knows his Lord through his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. He does not hesitate, when in distress, to return to Him and ask Him to remove his affliction. As He said: وكان الإنسان كفورا "and man is very ungrateful" (17:67), and He said: إن الإنسان لظلوم كفار "Indeed man is most unfair and ungrateful" (14:34).

So His saying: "When distress befalls man, he supplicates his Lord, turning to Him penitently" (وإذا مس الإنسان ضر دعا ربه منيبا إليه). That is, when man is struck by distress from hardship, illness, famine, or the like, he supplicates his Lord—and He is Allah, whose Lordship he acknowledges at that moment—returning to Him and turning away from all others, asking Him to remove the distress from him.

His saying: ثم إذا خوله نعمة منه نسي ما كان يدعوا إليه من قبل "then, when He grants him a blessing from Himself, he forgets that for which he had supplicated Him before" (39:8). That is, when his Lord, the Glorified, gives him a blessing from Himself after removing the distress, he becomes preoccupied with it, completely absorbed, and forgets the distress that he was supplicating Him to remove before the blessing was given.

The word ma (ما) in His saying: "that for which he had supplicated Him" (ما كان يدعوا إليه) is a relative pronoun (موصولة) referring to the distress, and the pronoun in "to it" (إليه) refers to it. It has also been said that it is an infinitive (مصدرية) and the pronoun refers to the Lord, the Glorified; the meaning being: "he forgets his supplication to his Lord before the bestowal." It has also been said that it is a relative pronoun referring to Allah, the Glorified, but this is the most remote of views.

His saying: وجعل لله أندادا ليضل عن سبيله "and he sets up equals to Allah, to lead [people] astray from His way" (39:8). "Equals" (الأنداد) are peers, and what is meant by them—as has been said—are the idols and their lords. The lam (ل) in "to lead astray from His way" (ليضل عن سبيله) denotes the consequence (العاقبة). The meaning is: he takes peers for Allah who share with Him in Lordship and Divinity according to his claim, so that this ends in leading people astray from the way of Allah, because people are naturally inclined toward imitation (التقليد), following one another, and in the action itself is a call similar to speech.

It is not unlikely that "equals" refers to all causes (الأسباب) upon which man relies and in which he finds tranquility, among which are the lords of the idols for the pagan. This is because the verse describes "man," which is more general than the polytheist, though the specific case of the verse is the disbeliever.

His saying: قل تمتع بكفرك قليلا إنك من أصحاب النار "Say, ‘Enjoy your ungratefulness for a little while; you are indeed among the inmates of the Fire’" (39:8). That is, enjoy a little enjoyment that will not last for you, because you are among the inmates of the Fire; your destination is toward it. This is a threatening command in the sense of informing; i.e., you are headed to the Fire, and your enjoyment of disbelief for a few days will not repel it from you.

His saying, the Exalted: أمن هو قانت آناء الليل ساجدا وقائما يحذر الآخرة ويرجوا رحمة ربه "Is he who is obedient during the hours of the night, prostrating and standing, fearing the Hereafter and hoping for the mercy of his Lord..." (39:9). The verse is not without connection and link to His previous saying: "No bearer shall bear another’s burden," for its implication is that the disbeliever and the grateful are not equal and do not mix. So He clarified this in this verse by stating that the obedient one who fears the punishment and hopes for mercy is not equal to others.

So His saying: "Is he who is obedient during the hours of the night, prostrating and standing, fearing the Hereafter and hoping for the mercy of his Lord..." (أمن هو قانت آناء الليل ساجدا وقائما يحذر الآخرة ويرجوا رحمة ربه). One of the two sides of the hesitation is suppressed, and the estimation is: "Is this one whom We mentioned better, or he who is obedient...?" etc.

"Obedience" (القنوت)—as mentioned by al-Raghib—is the persistence in obedience with humility (الخضوع). "Hours" (الآناء) is the plural of ana, which is time. "Fearing the Hereafter" (يحذر الآخرة) means the punishment of Allah in the Hereafter. The Exalted said: إن عذاب ربك كان محذورا "Indeed the punishment of your Lord is to be dreaded" (17:57). His saying: "hoping for the mercy of his Lord" (يرجوا رحمة ربه), along with what precedes it, combines the fear of punishment and the hope for mercy. He did not restrict mercy to the Hereafter, for the mercy of the Hereafter may encompass the world.

The meaning is: Is this disbeliever who is among the inmates of the Fire better, or he who is persistent in obedience and humility to his Lord during the times of the night when it covers him, prostrating in his prayer at times and standing in it at others, fearing the punishment of the Hereafter and hoping for the mercy of his Lord? That is, they are not equal.

His saying: قل هل يستوي الذين يعلمون والذين لا يعلمون "Say, ‘Are those who know equal to those who do not know?’" (39:9). Knowledge and its absence are absolute here, but what is meant by them—according to what applies to the case of the verse—is knowledge of Allah and its absence. For that is what completes the human being and benefits them in the true sense of the word, and its absence harms them. Other forms of knowledge, like wealth, are benefited from in the life of this world and perish with its perishing.

His saying: إنما يتذكر أولوا الألباب "Only those who possess intellect take admonition" (39:9). That is, those possessed of minds. This is in the position of explaining the reason for the lack of equality between the two groups: that one of the two groups takes admonition from the realities of things while the other group does not; thus they are not equal. Rather, those who know are preferred over others.

His saying, the Exalted: قل يا عباد الذين آمنوا اتقوا ربكم للذين أحسنوا في هذه الدنيا حسنة "Say, ‘[Allah says:] O My servants who have faith! Be wary of your Lord. For those who do good in this world there will be a good [reward]’" (39:10). The prepositional phrase "in this world" (في هذه الدنيا) is related to His saying: "do good" (أحسنوا). So the intent of the sentence is a promise to those who do good—i.e., persist in righteous deeds—that they shall have a "good" (حسنة) whose description cannot be measured by any measure.

He left "good" absolute, not restricting it to this world or the Hereafter. Its apparent meaning includes this world; thus, for the doing-good believers in this world, there is goodness of soul, peace of spirit, and protection of the self from what the disbelievers fluctuate in of confusion of mind, division of heart, rancor of the breast, submission to apparent causes, and the loss of one who is hoped for in every calamity, aided by when the striker strikes, and in whom tranquility is found in every descent. In the Hereafter, there is eternal happiness and lasting bliss.

It has been said that "in this world" is related to "good," but that is not the case.

His saying: وأرض الله واسعة "and Allah’s earth is vast" (39:10). This is an incitement and encouragement for them to migrate (الهجرة) from Mecca, as staying there had become difficult for the believers in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the polytheists were increasing their pressure and persecution (الفتنة) upon them every day. The verse, according to its wording, is general.

It has been said that "Allah's earth" refers to Paradise; i.e., Paradise is vast and there is no crowding therein, so acquire it through obedience and worship. This is remote.

His saying: إنما يوفى الصابرون أجرهم بغير حساب "Indeed the patient will be paid their reward in full without any reckoning" (39:10). Paying in full (توفية الأجر) is to give it completely and perfectly. The context implies that the restriction in the discourse is directed toward His saying: "without any reckoning" (بغير حساب). So the prepositional phrase is related to His saying: "will be paid," acting as an attribute for an infinitive indicated by it. The meaning is: the patient (الصابرون) are not given their reward except as a giving without reckoning. Thus, the patient are not held to account for their deeds, no register (ديوان) is spread for them, and their reward is not measured by the weight of their deeds.

He left "the patient" absolute in the verse, not restricting it to patience in obedience, or from disobedience, or during calamity, although what applies to the case of the verse is patience during the calamities of the world, especially what befalls from the side of the people of disbelief and transgression against those who believe in Allah, make their religion sincere for Him, and are God-conscious of Him.

It has been said that "without any reckoning" is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "their reward," implying the abundance of the reward they are paid in full. The previous view is closer.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Yazid al-Raqashi: that a man said: "O Messenger of Allah, we give our wealth seeking mention; do we have any reward in that?" The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Indeed, Allah does not accept except from one who is sincere to Him." Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse: ألا لله الدين الخالص "Look! [Only] exclusive religion belongs to Allah."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir narrated through the path of Juwaybir from Ibn Abbas: regarding والذين اتخذوا من دونه أولياء "As for those who take guardians besides Him"—the verse—he said: "It was revealed concerning three tribes: 'Amir, Kinanah, and Bani Salamah. They used to worship idols and say: 'The angels are His daughters.' So they said: ما نعبدهم إلا ليقربونا إلى الله زلفى 'We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah in station.'"

I say: The verse is absolute, encompassing all pagans. The saying: "We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah in station" is the saying of all of them, as is the claim of offspring. There is no explicit mention in the verse of the claim that the angels are daughters; thus, the truth is that the report is an application (تطبيق) of the verse.

In al-Kafi and al-'Ilal, with their chains to Zurarah from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), he said: I asked about آناء الليل ساجدا وقائما "during the hours of the night, prostrating and standing" etc. He said: "It means the night prayer (صلاة الليل)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Him, the Mighty and Majestic: هل يستوي الذين يعلمون والذين لا يعلمون إنما يتذكر أولوا الألباب "Are those who know equal to those who do not know? Only those who possess intellect take admonition." He said: "We are those who know, our enemy is those who do not know, and our followers (شيعتنا) are those who possess intellect."

I say: This meaning is narrated through many paths from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon them), and it is an application (جري) and not part of the literal commentary (التفسير) at all.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Sa'd narrated in his Tabaqat and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn Abbas: regarding His saying: أمن هو قانت آناء الليل ساجدا وقائما "Is he who is obedient during the hours of the night, prostrating and standing," he said: "It was revealed concerning 'Ammar ibn Yasir."

I say: Something similar was narrated from Juwaybir from 'Ikrimah. It was also narrated from Juwaybir from Ibn Abbas: that it was revealed concerning Ibn Mas'ud, 'Ammar, and Salim the client of Abu Hudhayfah. It was narrated from Abu Nu'aym and Ibn 'Asakir from Ibn 'Umar that it was 'Uthman, and other things have been said. All of these are applications and not "revelation" in the technical sense (المعنى المصطلح عليه), as the Surah was revealed all at once.

In al-Majma', al-'Ayyashi narrated with a chain to 'Abdullah ibn Sinan from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When the registers are spread and the scales are set, no scale will be set for the people of affliction (أهل البلاء), nor will any register be spread for them." Then he recited this verse: إنما يوفى الصابرون أجرهم بغير حساب "Indeed the patient will be paid their reward in full without any reckoning."

I say: Something similar in meaning was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Anas ibn Malik from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in a tradition.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصًا لَّهُ ٱلدِّينَ (١١)
Say, ‘I have been commanded to worship Allah with exclusive faith in Him, (11)
وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (١٢)
and I have been commanded to be the foremost of those who submit [to Him].’ (12)
قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٣)
Say, ‘Indeed, should I disobey my Lord, I fear the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (13)
قُلِ ٱللَّهَ أَعْبُدُ مُخْلِصًا لَّهُۥ دِينِى (١٤)
Say, ‘I worship [only] Allah, putting my exclusive faith in Him. (14)
فَٱعْبُدُوا۟ مَا شِئْتُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ أَلَا ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْخُسْرَانُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٥)
You worship whatever you wish besides Him.’ Say, ‘The losers are those who ruin themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection.’ Indeed, that is a manifest loss! (15)
لَهُم مِّن فَوْقِهِمْ ظُلَلٌ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ وَمِن تَحْتِهِمْ ظُلَلٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يُخَوِّفُ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦ عِبَادَهُۥ ۚ يَـٰعِبَادِ فَٱتَّقُونِ (١٦)
There will be canopies of fire above them, and [similar] canopies beneath them. With that Allah deters His servants. So, My servants, be wary of Me! (16)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱجْتَنَبُوا۟ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ أَن يَعْبُدُوهَا وَأَنَابُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ لَهُمُ ٱلْبُشْرَىٰ ۚ فَبَشِّرْ عِبَادِ (١٧)
As for those who stay clear of the worship of fake deities and turn penitently to Allah, there is good news for them. So give good news to My servants (17)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ ٱلْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (١٨)
who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [interpretation] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect. (18)
أَفَمَنْ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ كَلِمَةُ ٱلْعَذَابِ أَفَأَنتَ تُنقِذُ مَن فِى ٱلنَّارِ (١٩)
Can he against whom the word of punishment has become due…? Can you rescue someone who is in the Fire? (19)
لَـٰكِنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ رَبَّهُمْ لَهُمْ غُرَفٌ مِّن فَوْقِهَا غُرَفٌ مَّبْنِيَّةٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ (٢٠)
But as for those who are wary of their Lord, for them there will be lofty abodes with [other] lofty abodes built above them, with streams running beneath them—a promise of Allah. Allah does not break His promise. (20)

(Commentary)

In these verses, there is a return to the opening of the discourse, where the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is commanded to inform them that the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the sincerity of religion (إخلاص الدين) toward Allah to which he calls them is something he himself is commanded to uphold just like any one of them. Furthermore, he is commanded to be the first Muslim (أول مسلم) — the first to submit entirely to the divine will — regarding that which he calls toward; that is, he should be in a state of calling to that which he has already submitted to and believed in beforehand, whether they respond to his call or reject it.

They must, therefore, abandon any hope that his actions might contradict his words or that his conduct might oppose his call. For he is one who responds to his Lord, submits to Him, is steadfast in his religion, and fears Him should he disobey Him. The verses then warn the disbelievers (الكافرين) and give glad tidings to the believers (المؤمنين) regarding what Allah, the Glorified, has prepared for each of the two groups in terms of punishment or blessing.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللهَ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ الدِّينَ "Say, ‘I have been commanded to worship Allah, dedicating my religion purely to Him" until His saying: أَوَّلَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ "the first of the Muslims" (39:11-12) is a return of sorts to His saying, the Exalted, at the opening of the Surah: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ فَاعْبُدِ اللهَ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ الدِّينَ "Indeed We have sent down the Book to you with the truth; so worship Allah, dedicating your religion purely to Him" (39:2). The purpose is to make them despair of influencing him, so they do not hope that he might abandon his call or agree with them in associating partners with Allah (الإشراك بالله), as alluded to in the beginning of Surah Sad and other verses.

It is as if He is saying: Tell them that what I have recited to you regarding the command of the Exalted to worship Him with sincerity of religion — a command addressed directly to me — does not merely mean that I am calling you to it while I stand in the position of the listener in the discourse (as in the proverb, "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor"). Rather, I am like any one of you, commanded to worship Him with sincerity of religion. Not only that, but I am commanded to be the first of the Muslims (أول المسلمين) regarding the revelation (الوحي) sent down to me; thus, I submit to it first and then convey it to others. I fear my Lord and worship Him with sincerity whether you believe in Him or disbelieve; so do not place your hopes in me.

Thus, His saying: قُلْ إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللهَ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ الدِّينَ "Say, ‘I have been commanded to worship Allah, dedicating my religion purely to Him" indicates that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله shares with others the general command of worship, even without the mention of sincerity.

And His saying: وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ "And I have been commanded to be the first of the Muslims" indicates that in the command directed toward him, there is an addition to the obligation (التكليف) directed toward you. This is because I was commanded with what I was commanded, and the discourse was directed to me before you, and the purpose of it is that I be the first to submit to this command and believe in it.

It has been said that the lam in His saying: لِأَنْ أَكُونَ "to be" is for causality (التعليل), meaning: "I was commanded with that so that I might be the first of the Muslims." It has also been said that the lam is extra (زائدة), just as the lam was omitted in His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ "Say, ‘I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit’" (6:14).

The outcome of both views is the same in terms of meaning. For his being صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله the first of the Muslims provides a title for his submission (إسلامه), and the title of an act can validly be made the goal of the command for that act, or it can be made the object of the command itself. For example, one might say, "Strike him for the sake of discipline," or "Discipline him by striking."

The author of al-Kashshaf said: "There are several aspects to its meaning: that I be the first to submit in my time and from my people, because he was the first to oppose the religion of his forefathers and to cast off and smash the idols; or that I be the first of those whom I called to Islam to actually submit; or that I be the first to call my own soul to that which I call others, so that I may be an exemplar in both my speech and my action, and my attribute not be like that of kings who command what they do not do; or that I perform that which merits primacy through the deeds of the foremost (السابقين), indicating the cause by the effect." End of quote.

As you are aware, the most appropriate for the context of the verses is the third aspect, which is what we presented, and the other aspects follow from it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنِّي أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ "Say, ‘I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous day’" (39:13). The meaning of "disobeying his Lord," as evidenced by the context, is to oppose His command to worship Him with sincerity of religion. The "tremendous day" refers to the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة). The verse serves as a preamble to the content of the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللهَ أَعْبُدُ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ دِينِي فَاعْبُدُوا ما شِئْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ "Say, ‘[Only] Allah do I worship, dedicating my religion purely to Him. So worship whatever you wish besides Him’" (39:14-15). This is an explicit declaration that he is complying with the command of his Lord and obeying Him, after it was alluded to in the previous verse. It serves to make them despair of any hope that he might disobey the command of his Lord.

The advancement of the object (تقديم المفعول) in His saying: قُلِ اللهَ أَعْبُدُ "Say, ‘[Only] Allah do I worship’" signifies exclusivity (الحصر). His saying: مُخْلِصاً لَهُ دِينِي "dedicating my religion purely to Him" emphasizes this meaning of exclusivity. His saying: فَاعْبُدُوا ما شِئْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ "So worship whatever you wish besides Him" is a command of threat (تهديدي), meaning that such worship will not benefit them, for the evil consequence of their turning away from the sincere worship of Allah will surely strike them, as indicated by the end of the verse: قُلْ إِنَّ الْخاسِرِينَ "Say, ‘The losers...’" etc.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّ الْخاسِرِينَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ "Say, ‘The losers are those who lose themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection’" (39:15). Loss (الخسر) and perdition (الخسران) refer to the depletion of capital, either entirely or partially. Perdition (الخسران) is more emphatic than loss (الخسر). The perdition of the self (خسران النفس) is to lead it into the place of destruction and wretchedness such that its capacity for perfection is nullified, and thus happiness eludes it to the point that it can no longer be hoped for. The same applies to the loss of one's family.

In the verse, there is an allusion to the polytheists addressed by His saying: فَاعْبُدُوا ما شِئْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ "So worship whatever you wish besides Him." It is as if He is saying: Whatever you worship, you are losing yourselves by leading them through disbelief (الكفر) into the place of destruction, and your families — who are your kin — by leading them toward disbelief and polytheism (الشرك); and this is the true perdition.

His saying: أَلا ذلِكَ هُوَ الْخُسْرانُ الْمُبِينُ "Look! That is the manifest loss" (39:15). This is because loss related to the world — such as loss in wealth or status — is fleeting and comes to an end, unlike the loss of the Day of Resurrection, which is permanent and eternal, having no end or cessation.

Furthermore, if wealth or status vanishes through loss, it is possible for it to be replaced by something similar or better, unlike the self when it is lost.

This is based on the assumption that "family" (الأهل) refers to a person's kin in the world. It has also been said that "family" refers to those whom Allah had prepared in Paradise for the person had he believed and been God-conscious — such as spouses, servants, and others. This is more plausible and more appropriate to the context, for lineage and every worldly social bond are severed on the Day of Resurrection. The Exalted says: فَلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ "there will be no ties of kinship between them on that day" (23:101), and He says: يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً "the day when no soul will be of any avail to another soul" (82:19), among other verses.

This is also supported by His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَسَوْفَ يُحاسَبُ حِساباً يَسِيراً وَيَنْقَلِبُ إِلى أَهْلِهِ مَسْرُوراً "As for him who is given his record in his right hand, he will receive an easy reckoning, and he will return to his folks joyfully" (84:7-9).

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ ظُلَلٌ مِنَ النَّارِ وَمِنْ تَحْتِهِمْ ظُلَلٌ "They will have layers of fire above them, and layers of fire below them" (39:16). Layers (الظلل) is the plural of zullah, which — as has been said — is a high covering.

The meaning of these being above them and below them is their being encompassed by them, for fire is typically associated with these two directions; the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ اجْتَنَبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ أَنْ يَعْبُدُوها وَأَنابُوا إِلَى اللهِ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى "As for those who stay clear of the rebel [gods] (الطاغوت) lest they should worship them, and turn penitently to Allah, there are good tidings for them" (39:17). Al-Raghib said: "The rebel god (الطاغوت) is an expression for every transgressor and every object of worship besides Allah; it is used for both the singular and the plural." End of quote. It appears that what is meant by it in the verse are idols and every rebellious object of worship besides Allah.

He did not limit it to merely avoiding the worship of the rebel god, but added to it His saying: وَأَنَابُوا إِلَى اللهِ "and turn penitently to Allah," indicating that mere negation (النفي) avails nothing. Rather, what benefits a human being is the combination of negation and affirmation (الإثبات): the worship of Allah and the abandonment of the worship of others, which is to worship Him with sincerity of religion.

His saying: لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى "there are good tidings for them" is the proclamation of glad tidings and the predicate (خبر) for His saying: وَالَّذِينَ اجْتَنَبُوا "As for those who stay clear..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: فَبَشِّرْ عِبادِ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ "So give good tidings to My servants who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [interpretation] of it" (39:17-18) until the end of the verse. The apparent requirement of the context was to say "so give them good tidings," but instead it said "so give good tidings to My servants," adding the possessive pronoun of the first person to honor them and to describe them with His saying: الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ "those who listen to the word" etc.

What is meant by "the word" (القول), by the evidence of the mention of "following" (الاتباع), is that which has some connection and relevance to action. Thus, the "best word" is the one most guided in attaining the truth and most sincere in its advice to the human being. When a human being is one who loves beauty (الحسن) and is attracted to elegance, then the more the beauty increases, the more his attraction increases. If he finds something ugly and something beautiful, he leans toward the beautiful; and if he finds something beautiful and something even better (أحسن), he seeks that which is better. However, if he does not lean toward the better and remains fixed on the merely beautiful, it reveals that he is not attracted to it by virtue of its beauty; otherwise, the attraction would increase with the increase of beauty.

Thus, describing them as following the best word means they are naturally disposed toward seeking the truth (الحق), desiring right-guidance (الرشد), and attaining reality. Whenever the matter revolves between truth and falsehood, or guidance and error, they follow truth and guidance and abandon falsehood and error. And whenever the matter revolves between the truth and that which is more true (الأحق), or guidance and that which is more guided, they take that which is more true and more guided.

Truth and guidance are their objective; therefore, they listen to the word and do not reject any word as soon as it strikes their ears by following their own whims without contemplating it or understanding it.

Thus, the meaning of His saying: الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ "who listen to the word and follow the best of it" is that they are seekers of truth and guidance, listening to the word in the hope of finding truth within it and out of fear that any part of it might elude them.

It has been said: What is meant by listening to the word and following the best of it is listening to the Quran and other things and following the Quran. It has also been said: What is meant is listening to the commands of Allah the Exalted and following the best of them, such as retaliation and forgiveness — so they follow forgiveness; or the publicizing of charity and its concealment — so they follow concealment. Both views are instances of specification (التخصيص) without a specifier.

His saying: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَداهُمُ اللهُ "They are the ones whom Allah has guided" (39:18) indicates that this attribute is the divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية). This guidance — namely, the seeking of truth and the complete readiness to follow the truth wherever it is found — is the general guidance (الهداية الإجمالية), and to it every detailed guidance (هداية تفصيلية) toward divine gnosis returns.

His saying: وَأُولئِكَ هُمْ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ "and it is they who are possessors of intellect" (39:18); that is, those who possess minds. From this, it is understood that the intellect (العقل) is that by which one is guided to the truth, and its sign is the attribute of following the truth. It has already been mentioned in the commentary on His saying: وَمَنْ يَرْغَبُ عَنْ مِلَّةِ إِبْراهِيمَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُ "And who will turn away from the creed of Abraham except one who befools himself?" (2:130) that it is understood from it that the intellect is that by which the religion of Allah is followed.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ كَلِمَةُ الْعَذابِ أَفَأَنْتَ تُنْقِذُ مَنْ فِي النَّارِ "Is he against whom the word of punishment has become due [to be delivered]? Can you rescue someone who is in the Fire?" (39:19). The establishment of the "word of punishment" (كلمة العذاب) is the necessity of entering the Fire due to disbelief, as per His saying when Adam was sent down to the earth: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ "As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (2:39) and similar verses.

The requirement of the context is that there is an ellipsis (إضمار) in the verse indicated by His saying: أَفَأَنْتَ تُنْقِذُ مَنْ فِي النَّارِ "Can you rescue someone who is in the Fire?". The estimated meaning is: "Is he against whom the word of punishment has become due [one who] escapes it?" This is better than estimating: "Is he... better, or he for whom Paradise has become mandatory?"

It has been said: The meaning is, "Is he against whom the threat of the Exalted with punishment has become mandatory — can you save him from the Fire?" Thus, the mention of مَنْ فِي النَّارِ "someone who is in the Fire" was sufficient to replace the pronoun referring back to the subject (المبتدأ), and the interrogative was brought twice for emphasis to draw attention to the meaning.

It has also been said: The estimated meaning is, "Can you rescue someone who is in the Fire from among them?" thus omitting the pronoun; this is the weakest of the views.

His saying, the Exalted: لكِنِ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ لَهُمْ غُرَفٌ مِنْ فَوْقِها غُرَفٌ مَبْنِيَّةٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ "But those who are wary of their Lord will have high-roofed halls, with high-roofed halls built over them, with rivers flowing over them" (39:20). High-roofed halls (الغرف) is the plural of ghurfah, which is an elevated dwelling. It has been said: This is in contrast to His saying regarding the disbelievers: لَهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ ظُلَلٌ مِنَ النَّارِ وَمِنْ تَحْتِهِمْ ظُلَلٌ "They will have layers of fire above them, and layers of fire below them."

His saying: وَعْدَ اللهِ "[This is] the promise of Allah" (39:20); that is, Allah promised them that as a promise. It is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) standing in place of its verb. His saying: لا يُخْلِفُ اللهُ الْمِيعادَ "Allah does not fail His promise" (39:20) is an announcement of His practice (سنة) regarding His promises, and in it is a comforting of their souls.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّ الْخاسِرِينَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ "Say, ‘The losers are those who lose themselves and their families’" (39:15), he said: "They have been cheated (غبنوا) of themselves and their families."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ اجْتَنَبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ أَنْ يَعْبُدُوها وَأَنابُوا إِلَى اللهِ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى "As for those who stay clear of the rebel [gods] lest they should worship them, and turn penitently to Allah, there are good tidings for them" (39:17): Abu Basir narrated from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام that he said: "You are they; and whoever obeys a tyrant (جبارا) has indeed worshipped him."

I say: This is an instance of conceptual application (الجري).

In al-Kafi: Some of our companions narrated in a marfu' fashion from Hisham ibn al-Hakam who said: Abu al-Hasan Musa ibn Ja'far عليه‌السلام said to me: "O Hisham, indeed Allah — Blessed and Exalted is He — gave glad tidings to the people of intellect and understanding in His Book, saying: فَبَشِّرْ عِبادِ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَداهُمُ اللهُ وَأُولئِكَ هُمْ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ 'So give good tidings to My servants who listen to the word and follow the best of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who are possessors of intellect.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim extracted from Zayd ibn Aslam regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ اجْتَنَبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ أَنْ يَعْبُدُوها "As for those who stay clear of the rebel [gods] lest they should worship them" (39:17), he said: "These two verses were revealed concerning three individuals who, during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), used to say 'There is no god but Allah': Zayd ibn 'Amr ibn Nufayl, Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, and Salman al-Farisi."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma' from 'Abdullah ibn Zayd. It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn 'Umar that it was revealed concerning Sa'id ibn Zayd, Abu Dharr, and Salman. It was also narrated from Juwaybir from Jabir ibn 'Abdillah that it was revealed concerning a man from the Ansar who emancipated seven slaves when His saying, the Exalted: لَها سَبْعَةُ أَبْوابٍ "It has seven gates" (15:44) was revealed. It appears that all of these are instances of applying the story to the verse.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَسَلَكَهُۥ يَنَـٰبِيعَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ يُخْرِجُ بِهِۦ زَرْعًا مُّخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَٰنُهُۥ ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَرَىٰهُ مُصْفَرًّا ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُۥ حُطَـٰمًا ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٢١)
Have you not seen that Allah sends down water from the sky, then He conducts it through the ground as springs. Then He brings forth with it crops of diverse hues. Then they wither and you see them turn yellow. Then He turns them into chaff. There is indeed a lesson in that for those who possess intellect. (21)
أَفَمَن شَرَحَ ٱللَّهُ صَدْرَهُۥ لِلْإِسْلَـٰمِ فَهُوَ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۚ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلْقَـٰسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُم مِّن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٢)
Is someone whose breast Allah has opened to Islam so that he follows a light from His Lord…? So woe to those whose hearts have been hardened to the remembrance of Allah. They are in manifest error. (22)
ٱللَّهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْحَدِيثِ كِتَـٰبًا مُّتَشَـٰبِهًا مَّثَانِىَ تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ ثُمَّ تَلِينُ جُلُودُهُمْ وَقُلُوبُهُمْ إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ يَهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ (٢٣)
Allah has sent down the best of discourses, a scripture [composed] of similar motifs, whereat shiver the skins of those who fear their Lord, then their skins and hearts relax at Allah’s remembrance. That is Allah’s guidance, by which He guides whomever He wishes; and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide. (23)
أَفَمَن يَتَّقِى بِوَجْهِهِۦ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ وَقِيلَ لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ ذُوقُوا۟ مَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ (٢٤)
What! Is someone who fends off with his face the terrible punishment [meted out to him] on the Day of Resurrection…? And the wrongdoers will be told, ‘Taste what you used to earn.’ (24)
كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٢٥)
Those who were before them impugned [the apostles], whereat the punishment overtook them whence they were not aware. (25)
فَأَذَاقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخِزْىَ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٦)
So Allah made them taste disgrace in the life of the world, and the punishment of the Hereafter will surely be greater, if they knew. (26)
وَلَقَدْ ضَرَبْنَا لِلنَّاسِ فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٧)
We have drawn for mankind in this Quran every [kind of] example, so that they may take admonition: (27)
قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا غَيْرَ ذِى عِوَجٍ لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (٢٨)
an Arabic Quran, without any deviousness, so that they may be Godwary. (28)
ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا رَّجُلًا فِيهِ شُرَكَآءُ مُتَشَـٰكِسُونَ وَرَجُلًا سَلَمًا لِّرَجُلٍ هَلْ يَسْتَوِيَانِ مَثَلًا ۚ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٩)
Allah draws an example: a man jointly owned by several contending masters, and a man belonging entirely to one man: are the two equal in comparison? All praise belongs to Allah! But most of them do not know. (29)
إِنَّكَ مَيِّتٌ وَإِنَّهُم مَّيِّتُونَ (٣٠)
You will die, and they [too] will die. (30)
ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ تَخْتَصِمُونَ (٣١)
Then on the Day of Resurrection you will contend before your Lord. (31)
۞ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَذَبَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وَكَذَّبَ بِٱلصِّدْقِ إِذْ جَآءَهُۥٓ ۚ أَلَيْسَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوًى لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٢)
So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who attributes a falsehood to Allah, and denies the truth when it reaches him? Is not the [final] abode of the faithless in hell? (32)
وَٱلَّذِى جَآءَ بِٱلصِّدْقِ وَصَدَّقَ بِهِۦٓ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ (٣٣)
He who brings the truth and he who confirms it—it is they who are the Godwary. (33)
لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣٤)
They will have what they wish with their Lord—that is the reward of the virtuous— (34)
لِيُكَفِّرَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ أَسْوَأَ ٱلَّذِى عَمِلُوا۟ وَيَجْزِيَهُمْ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٣٥)
that Allah may absolve them of the worst of what they did, and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do. (35)
أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِكَافٍ عَبْدَهُۥ ۖ وَيُخَوِّفُونَكَ بِٱلَّذِينَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ (٣٦)
Does not Allah suffice [to defend] His servant? They would frighten you of others than Him. Yet whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide, (36)
وَمَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن مُّضِلٍّ ۗ أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِعَزِيزٍ ذِى ٱنتِقَامٍ (٣٧)
and whomever Allah guides, there is no one who can lead him astray. Is not Allah an all-mighty avenger? (37)

(Commentary)

This is a return to the beginning of the argument for His Lordship (الربوبية), the Exalted, and a discourse on the guidance of the guided and the straying of the lost, drawing a comparison between the two groups and the ultimate end of each, while also explaining the nature of the Quran’s guidance.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسَلَكَهُ يَنابِيعَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — Have you not regarded that Allah sends down water from the sky and guides it into the earth’s springs? (39:21) until the end of the verse.
