It is stated in al-Majma‘: "The springs (الينابيع) is the plural of yanbu‘, which is that from which water gushes forth; it is said 'the water gushed (نبع) from such a place' when it wells up from it. The crop (الزرع) refers to that which grows without a stalk, while the tree (الشجر) is that which possesses a stalk and branches; 'plant' (النبات) encompasses both. The plant 'withers' (هاج) when it dries up and reaches its ultimate state of desiccation. Chaff (الحطام) refers to the fragments of straw and dry grass." End quote.

His saying: فَسَلَكَهُ يَنابِيعَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — and guides it into the earth’s springs means He causes it to enter into springs and channels within the earth, which are like veins in bodies, transporting what they carry from one side to another. The rest of the verse is clear, and the verse—as you see—argues for His uniqueness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية).

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ فَهُوَ عَلى نُورٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ فَوَيْلٌ لِلْقاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ مِنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ — Is he whose breast Allah has opened to Islam so that he follows a light from his Lord [like him who is hardhearted]? So woe to those whose hearts are hardened against the remembrance of Allah! (39:22) etc. Since He mentioned in the previous verse that in the sending down of water and the growth of plants there is a reminder (ذكرى) for those possessors of intellect (أولي الألباب)—who are His God-conscious (المتقون) servants, and He had mentioned before that they are the ones whom Allah has guided—He mentions in this verse that they are not like others among the straying (الضالين). He clarifies the reason for this: they follow a light (نور) from their Lord by which they perceive the truth, and in their hearts is a softness that does not refuse to accept the best of speech (أحسن القول) presented to them.

Thus, His saying: أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ — Is he whose breast Allah has opened has a deleted predicate (خبر) indicated by His saying: فَوَيْلٌ لِلْقاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ — So woe to those whose hearts are hardened etc., meaning: "Is he like those whose hearts are hardened?" The interrogative is for denial (الإنكار), meaning they are not equal.

The opening of the breast (شرح الصدر) is its expansion to encompass the speech presented to it. Since this is for Islam (الإسلام)—which is submission (التسليم) to Allah in what He wills, and that is nothing but the truth—its meaning is that the human being becomes such that he accepts the truthful speech presented to him and does not reject it. This is not an acceptance without understanding or in any random manner, but rather stems from insight (بصيرة) into the truth and recognition of right-guidance (الرشد). Therefore, He followed it with His saying: فَهُوَ عَلى نُورٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ — so that he follows a light from his Lord. He depicts him, by way of metaphor, as one riding upon a light by which he travels and perceives the truth that passes through the vast arena of his breast, seeing it and distinguishing it from falsehood. This is in contrast to the straying person, whose breast has no expansion to encompass the truth, nor is he riding a light from his Lord to perceive the truth and distinguish it.

His saying: فَوَيْلٌ لِلْقاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ مِنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ — So woe to those whose hearts are hardened against the remembrance of Allah is a derivation from the preceding sentence, indicating that those with hardened hearts—and the hardness (قساوة) and rigidity of the heart is a necessary consequence of the lack of the breast's expansion and the absence of light—do not take heed of the signs (آيات) of Allah and thus are not guided to the truth they indicate. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: أُولئِكَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ — They are in manifest error. (39:22).

In this verse, guidance (الهداية) is defined by its necessary consequence, which is the opening of the breast (شرح الصدر) and being upon a light from one's Lord. Similarly, straying (الضلال) is defined by its necessary consequence, which is the hardening of the heart (قساوة القلب) against the remembrance of Allah.

We have already discussed the meaning of guidance in the commentary on His saying: فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ — Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam (6:125); so refer back to it.

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ — Allah has sent down the best of discourses as a scripture composed of similar motifs, whereat shiver (39:23) until the end of the verse. This is like a summary after the detail regarding the previous verse, considering what is gained from the verse concerning the meaning of guidance, even though it is an exposition of the guidance of the Quran.

His saying: اللهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ — Allah has sent down the best of discourses refers to the Noble Quran. Discourse (الحديث) is speech (القول), as in His saying: فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ — let them bring a discourse like it (52:34), and His saying: فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَهُ يُؤْمِنُونَ — so in what discourse after it will they believe? (77:50). It is the best of speech because it contains pure truth (الحق) which falsehood cannot approach from before it or from behind it; it is His Majestic Speech.

His saying: كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً — as a scripture composed of similar motifs means its parts resemble one another. This is different from the "similarity" (التشابه) found in the "ambiguous verses" (المتشابه) as opposed to the "clear verses" (المحكم), for that is a quality of some verses of the Book, while this is a quality of the whole.

His saying: مَثانِيَ — whereat shiver is the plural of mathniyyah, meaning "folded" or "turned back" (المعطوف), because its verses turn back toward one another and refer to one another, with some parts clarifying others and some interpreting others without any discrepancy, such that one part does not repel or contradict another. As He, the Exalted, said: أَفَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ غَيْرِ اللهِ لَوجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلافاً كَثِيراً — Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it. (4:82).

His saying: تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ — whereat shiver the skins of those who fear their Lord is an attribute of the Book and not a new sentence. Shivering (الاقشعرار) is the intense contraction of the skin due to a fear (خشية) arising from hearing or seeing something formidable. This only occurs because they possess insight into the standing of their souls before the greatness of their Lord; when they hear His speech, they turn toward the presence of Greatness and Majesty, so fear (الخشية) envelops their hearts and their skins begin to shiver.

His saying: ثُمَّ تَلِينُ جُلُودُهُمْ وَقُلُوبُهُمْ إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ — then their skins and hearts soften to Allah’s remembrance (39:23). The word "soften" (تَلِينُ) implies the meaning of tranquility and peace (السكون والطمأنينة), which is why it is followed by the preposition "to" (إلى). The meaning is: then their skins and hearts find rest and tranquility in the remembrance of Allah, becoming soft and accepting of it, or softening for it while finding peace in it.

He did not mention the hearts in the previous phrase when mentioning the shivering because hearts here refer to the souls (النفوس), and they do not shiver; rather, they experience fear (الخشية).

His saying: ذلِكَ هُدَى اللهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ — That is Allah’s guidance: He guides with it whomever He wishes (39:23). That is, the shivering of the skins that overcomes them from the Quran, followed by the tranquility of their skins and hearts in the remembrance of Allah, is the guidance of Allah. This is another definition of guidance (الهداية) by its necessary consequence.

His saying: يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ — He guides with it whomever He wishes means He guides with His guidance whomever He wills of His servants—those whose capacity (استعداد) for being guided has not been nullified and who have not been occupied by obstacles such as transgression (الفسق) and injustice (الظلم). The context suggests that guidance is from His grace (فضل) and is not a necessity that He is compelled to perform.

It has been said that the referent of "That" (ذلِكَ) in His saying ذلِكَ هُدَى اللهِ is the Quran, but this is as you see [unlikely]. Some have used these verses to argue that guidance is solely the work of Allah and no one else shares it with Him. The truth is that the verses are devoid of such an indication, even though it is true that guidance belongs to Allah, the Glorified, essentially (أصالة), and to those of His servants He chooses for it subordinately (تبعاً), as is understood from verses like: قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى اللهِ هُوَ الْهُدى — Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is the [true] guidance’ (2:120), and: إِنَّ عَلَيْنا لَلْهُدى — Indeed it is up to Us to guide (92:12), and: وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا — We made them imams, guiding by Our command (21:73), and: وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — and indeed you guide to a straight path (42:52).

Thus, all guidance belongs to Allah, either without a medium or through the medium of the guided guides (الهداة المهديين) among His creation. Accordingly, whomever He leads astray among His creation—by not guiding them through a medium or without one—has no guide. This is His saying at the end of the verse: وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ — and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide. (39:23). This phrase will appear again after several verses and is repeated throughout His speech, the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ يَتَّقِي بِوَجْهِهِ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَقِيلَ لِلظَّالِمِينَ ذُوقُوا ما كُنْتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ — Is he who shields himself with his face from the terrible punishment on the Day of Resurrection [like him who is oblivious to it]? And the wrongdoers will be told, ‘Taste what you used to earn.’ (39:24). This is a comparison between the people of punishment on the Day of Resurrection and those secure from it. These two groups are the people of straying and the people of guidance, which is why this verse follows the previous one.

The interrogative is for denial, and the predicate of "he who" (فَمَنْ) is deleted; the estimation is: "Is he like one who is in security from it?" "The Day of Resurrection" is connected to "shields himself." The meaning is: Is he who shields himself with his face from the terrible punishment on the Day of Resurrection—because his hands, with which he used to shield himself from harms, are shackled to his neck—like one who is secure from punishment and whom no harm touches? So it has been said.

It has also been said: Shielding with the face in the aforementioned sense is groundless because the face is not something one shields with; rather, the meaning is shielding with his entire self or specifically with his face from the terrible punishment of the Day of Resurrection, where "Day of Resurrection" is a qualifier for the punishment. This would be the opposite of the previous interpretation, meaning: Is he who shields himself from the terrible punishment of the Day of Resurrection while in this world through the God-consciousness (تقوى) of Allah like the one who persists in his disbelief? This is not free from affectation.

His saying: وَقِيلَ لِلظَّالِمِينَ ذُوقُوا ما كُنْتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ — And the wrongdoers will be told, ‘Taste what you used to earn’ is the speech of the angels of the Fire. It appears the sentence assumes the particle qad (قد) or otherwise, and the original would be "and it was said to them: Taste..." but the explicit noun "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين) was placed instead of the pronoun to indicate the cause of the judgment, which is injustice (الظلم).

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتاهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — Those who were before them denied; so the punishment came on them from where they were not aware. (39:25). That is, from a direction they did not expect, so they were surprised and seized while heedless, which is the severest of seizures. In this verse and the one following it is an exposition of the punishment of disgrace (عذاب الخزي) that befell some of the disbelievers, to serve as a lesson for others.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَذاقَهُمُ اللهُ الْخِزْيَ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَلَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ — So Allah made them taste disgrace in the life of the world, and the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known. (39:26). Disgrace (الخزي) is humiliation and belittlement. Allah made them taste it through various forms of punishment He sent down upon them, such as drowning, being swallowed by the earth, the Cry (الصيحة), the earthquake (الرجفة), metamorphosis (المسخ), and being killed.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ ضَرَبْنا لِلنَّاسِ فِي هذَا الْقُرْآنِ مِنْ كُلِّ مَثَلٍ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ — We have certainly drawn for mankind in this Quran every [kind of] parable, so that they may take admonition. (39:27). That is, We have set forth for them something of every type of parable so that they might be alerted, take heed, and be exhorted by remembering what they contain.

His saying, the Exalted: قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا غَيْرَ ذِي عِوجٍ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ — an Arabic Quran, without any crookedness, so that they may be God-conscious. (39:28). Crookedness (العوج) is deviation and bending. "An Arabic Quran" (قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا) is in the accusative case for praise, assuming "I praise" or "I specify" and the like, or it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) based on the description.

His saying, the Exalted: ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً رَجُلاً فِيهِ شُرَكاءُ مُتَشاكِسُونَ وَرَجُلاً سَلَماً لِرَجُلٍ هَلْ يَسْتَوِيانِ — Allah draws a parable: a man jointly owned by several contending masters, and a man belonging entirely to one man: are the two equal? (39:29) etc. Al-Raghib said: "al-shaks—with fath then kasr—is one of bad character. His saying: شُرَكاءُ مُتَشاكِسُونَ means they are quarreling due to the badness of their character." End quote. They interpreted "entirely" (سَلَماً) as being exclusive, not shared by many.

This is a parable Allah has drawn for the polytheist (المشرك) who worships different lords and deities who share him while they are in conflict; one commands him with what another forbids, and each wants to possess him exclusively and devote him to his own service. And for the monotheist (الموحد) who is exclusive to one master who has no partner, so he serves him in what he desires without any conflict leading to confusion. Thus, the polytheist is the man owned by contending partners, and the monotheist is the man belonging entirely to one man. They are not equal; rather, the one who belongs entirely to one man is in a better state than his counterpart.

This is a simple parable understandable to the general public, but upon closer scrutiny, it refers back to His saying, the Exalted: لَوْ كانَ فِيهِما آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللهُ لَفَسَدَتا — Had there been any gods in them besides Allah, they would have surely fallen apart (21:22), and it becomes a proof for the negation of the plurality of lords and deities.

His saying: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ — All praise belongs to Allah (39:29) is praise for Allah because His servitude (عبودية) is better than the servitude of anyone else.

His saying: بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — But most of them do not know (39:29) the merit of His worship over the worship of others, despite its complete clarity to anyone with the slightest insight.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكَ مَيِّتٌ وَإِنَّهُمْ مَيِّتُونَ ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عِنْدَ رَبِّكُمْ تَخْتَصِمُونَ — You will surely die, and they [too] will surely die. Then on the Day of Resurrection you will dispute before your Lord. (39:30-31). The first verse is a preamble to what is mentioned in the second regarding their disputing on the Day of Resurrection before their Lord. The address in "you" (إِنَّكُمْ) is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his community, or specifically the polytheists among them. Disputing (الاختصام)—as in al-Majma‘—is for each of two parties to reject what the other has brought by way of denial.

The meaning is: Your end and their end is death, then all of you on the Day of Resurrection, after appearing before your Lord, will dispute. Part of what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) will present has been recounted: وَقالَ الرَّسُولُ يا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُوراً — And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion.’ (25:30).

The two verses are general in their wording, but the four following verses support that the intended dispute is what occurs between the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the disbelievers of his community on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ كَذَبَ عَلَى اللهِ وَكَذَّبَ بِالصِّدْقِ إِذْ جاءَهُ أَلَيْسَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوىً لِلْكافِرِينَ — So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who lies against Allah and denies the truth when it comes to him? Is not the abode of the faithless in hell? (39:32). In this verse and those following it, there is a prompt mention of the ultimate end of their dispute on the Day of Resurrection and an allusion to the result of the judgment between them. It is as if it were said: The result of what will be judged between you is known today—who among you is the saved and who is the perished? For the judgment on that day revolves around injustice (الظلم) and excellence (الإحسان); there is no one more unjust than the disbeliever, while the believer is a God-conscious (متق) doer of good (محسن), and injustice leads to the Fire while excellence leads to Paradise. This is what the context provides.

Thus, His saying: فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ كَذَبَ عَلَى اللهِ — So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who lies against Allah means he who fabricates against Him by claiming He has partners. Injustice (الظلم) increases in magnitude according to the magnitude of the one it concerns; since it concerns Allah, the Glorified, it is greater than all injustice, and its perpetrator is more unjust than any other wrongdoer.

His saying: وَكَذَّبَ بِالصِّدْقِ إِذْ جاءَهُ — and denies the truth when it comes to him. What is meant by "the truth" (الصدق) is the truthful news, which is the divine religion brought by the Messenger, as evidenced by His saying: "when it comes to him."

His saying: أَلَيْسَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوىً لِلْكافِرِينَ — Is not the abode of the faithless in hell? The "abode" (المثوى) is a noun of place meaning dwelling and residence. The interrogative is for affirmation (التقرير), meaning that in Hell is the residence of these wrongdoers due to their arrogance toward the truth, which led to their fabrication against Allah and their denial of the truthful news brought by the Messenger.

The verse is specific to the polytheists of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) time or the polytheists of his community according to the context, but it is general for everyone who introduces an innovation (بدعة) and abandons a tradition (سنة) from the traditions of the religion.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِي جاءَ بِالصِّدْقِ وَصَدَّقَ بِهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُتَّقُونَ — But he who brings the truth and believes in it—it is they who are the God-conscious. (39:33). Bringing the truth refers to bringing the true religion, and believing in it refers to faith in it and in what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) brought.

His saying: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُتَّقُونَ — it is they who are the God-conscious. Perhaps the reference to "he who brings" is in the plural form because it is plural in meaning, encompassing every prophet who brought the true religion and believed in what he brought, and indeed every believer who believed in the true religion and called to it. For calling to the truth in word and deed is among the affairs of the followers of the Prophet. He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي — Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me.’ (12:108).

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — They shall have whatever they wish with their Lord; that is the reward of the virtuous (39:34). This is their reward with their Lord: they shall have whatever their will (مشيتهم) attaches to. The will there is the complete cause for the attainment of whatever a person wishes, whatever it may be, unlike the case in this world where the attainment of any life goal depends—in addition to the will—on many factors and causes, including effort and action derived from society and cooperation.

The verse indicates, first, their residence in the Abode of Proximity and the neighborhood of the Lord of the worlds; and second, that they shall have whatever they wish. These two are the reward of the God-conscious (المتقين), who are the virtuous (المحسنون). Their virtue (إحسان) is the reason for being given the aforementioned reward. This is the point in placing the explicit noun instead of the pronoun in His saying: ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — that is the reward of the virtuous, whereas the apparent requirement was to say: "and that is their reward."

Describing them with virtue (الإحسان)—the outward meaning of which is righteous deeds or both true belief and good deeds—testifies that the intended "belief" (التصديق) mentioned is belief in word and deed. Furthermore, the Quran does not call one who abandons some of what Allah has sent down of laws a "believer" in them.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيُكَفِّرَ اللهُ عَنْهُمْ أَسْوَأَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا — so that Allah may absolve them of the worst of what they did (39:35) until the end of the verse. It is well known that if He absolves their worst deeds, He absolves what is less than that. What is meant by "the worst of what they did" are things like polytheism and major sins.

It is stated in Majma‘ al-Bayan regarding the verse: "That is, Allah dropped from them the punishment for polytheism and the acts of disobedience they had committed before that, due to their faith, virtue, and return to Allah, the Exalted." End quote. This is good in terms of generalizing the evil deeds and in terms of restricting the absolution (التكفير) to what occurred before that through faith, virtue, and repentance. For the verse explains the effect of believing in the truth that came to them, which is the absolution of sins through belief and the good reward in the Hereafter.

And His saying: وَيَجْزِيَهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ الَّذِي كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — and reward them their wages by the best of what they used to do. (39:35).

It has been said: The meaning is that He looks at their deeds and rewards them for the best of them with a reward befitting it, and for what is not the best, He rewards them with the reward of the best. Thus, the "by" (بـ) is for exchange, like "I sold this for (بـ) this."

It could also be said: The meaning is that He looks at the highest of their deeds in degree, and their degree is raised accordingly, so that nothing of the ultimate perfection reached by their deeds is lost. However, the application of a similar logic to the absolution of the "worst" is somewhat obscure.

It has also been said: The superlative forms in the verse, "the worst" (أَسْوَأَ) and "by the best" (بِأَحْسَنِ), are used for absolute increase without regard to a compared object, for all disobedience to Allah is "worst" and all obedience to Him is "best."

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِكافٍ عَبْدَهُ وَيُخَوِّفُونَكَ بِالَّذِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِ — Does not Allah suffice His servant? They would frighten you with those besides Him. (39:36). What is meant by "those besides Him" are their deities other than Allah, as understood from the context. What is meant by "the servant" (العبد) is the one whom Allah praised in the previous verses, and it includes the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) primarily.

The interrogative is for affirmation, and the meaning is that He suffices them. In this is an assurance for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) against their frightening him with their deities, and an allusion to His promise of sufficiency, as He stated explicitly in His saying: فَسَيَكْفِيكَهُمُ اللهُ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — Allah will suffice you against them, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (2:137).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ وَوَمَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ مُضِلٍ — Whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide. And whomever Allah guides, there is no one who can lead him astray. (39:36-37). These are two reciprocal sentences presented as universal principles; hence the use of the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة), which is an instance of placing the explicit noun instead of the pronoun.

Following His saying: أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِكافٍ — Does not Allah suffice etc. with His saying: وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ — Whomever [He] leads astray etc. is an indication that those who threaten will never be guided to faith, their efforts will never succeed, and they will never achieve their desire or wish against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), for Allah will never lead him astray since He has guided him.

His saying: أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِعَزِيزٍ ذِي انْتِقامٍ — Is not Allah all-mighty, wielder of retribution? (39:37) is an interrogative for affirmation, meaning He is indeed so. This is an evident justification for His saying: وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ — Whomever Allah leads astray etc., for His Might (عزته) and His being the Wielder of Retribution (ذو انتقام) require that He take retribution against those who reject the truth and persist in their disbelief, so He leads them astray and no guide can guide them, because He, the Exalted, is All-Mighty (عزيز) and no overcomer can defeat Him in what He wills. Likewise, if He guides one of His servants due to his God-consciousness and virtue, no one who leads astray can lead him astray.

In this justification is an indication that the leading astray (الإضلال) attributed to Allah, the Exalted, is that which is by way of recompense and retribution, and not an initial leading astray, as has been mentioned repeatedly.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Rawdat al-Wa‘izin, it is narrated: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited: أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ فَهُوَ عَلى نُورٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ — Is he whose breast Allah has opened to Islam so that he follows a light from his Lord [like him who is hardhearted]? (39:22). He said: 'Indeed, when light enters the heart, it expands and opens for it.'"

They said: "O Messenger of Allah, is there a sign by which this may be known?" He said: "Shunning the abode of delusion (التجافي عن دار الغرور), turning toward the abode of eternity (الإنابة إلى دار الخلود), and preparing for death before death descends."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud, from al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi from Ibn Umar, and from Ibn Jarir and others from Qatadah.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ — Is he whose breast Allah has opened—the verse—he said: "It was revealed concerning the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام)."

I say: The revelation of the Surah all at once does not harmonize with this, as mentioned in similar cases.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas: "They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, if only you would speak to us,' so there was revealed: اللهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ — Allah has sent down the best of discourses (39:23)."

I say: This is an instance of application (التطبيق).

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ — whereat shiver the skins—the verse—it is narrated from al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: إذا اقشعر جلد العبد من خشية الله ـ تحاتت عنه ذنوبه ـ كما يتحات عن الشجرة اليابسة ورقها — "When the skin of a servant shivers from the fear of Allah, his sins fall away from him just as leaves fall from a dry tree."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا غَيْرَ ذِي عِوجٍ — an Arabic Quran, without any crookedness (39:28), al-Daylami narrated in Musnad al-Firdaws from Anas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا غَيْرَ ذِي عِوجٍ — an Arabic Quran, without any crookedness: "He said: 'Uncreated.'"

I say: The verse resists being applied to this narration. We have already discussed the meaning of "speech" (الكلام) in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: تِلْكَ الرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ — These are the apostles, some of whom We gave an advantage over others (2:253) in the second volume of this book.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَرَجُلاً سَلَماً لِرَجُلٍ — and a man belonging entirely to one man (39:29), al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani narrated with his chain from Ali (عليه السلام) that he said: "I am that man who belongs entirely (السلم) to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

I say: It was also narrated from al-Ayyashi with his chain from Abu Khalid from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام). This is an instance of "running" (الجري) [application to a specific case], while the parable is general.

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عِنْدَ رَبِّكُمْ تَخْتَصِمُونَ — Then on the Day of Resurrection you will dispute before your Lord (39:31), Ibn Umar said: "We used to think this was about us and the People of the Two Books. We said: 'How shall we dispute when our Prophet is one and our Book is one?' Until I saw some of us striking the faces of others with the sword; then I knew it was revealed concerning us."

Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri said: "We used to say: 'Our Lord is one, our Prophet is one, and our religion is one—so what is this dispute?' When the Day of Siffin occurred and some of us attacked others with swords, we said: 'Yes, this is it.'"

I say: The first tradition was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through various paths from Ibn Umar with differences in wording but the same meaning. It was also narrated by several compilers of hadith from Ibrahim al-Nakha‘i. Something similar was narrated through two paths from al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam. The second tradition was narrated from Sa‘id ibn Mansur from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri.

These traditions contradict what has been narrated that the Companions were mujtahids who are rewarded whether they were correct or mistaken.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِي جاءَ بِالصِّدْقِ وَصَدَّقَ بِهِ — But he who brings the truth and believes in it (39:33), it was said: "The one who brought the truth is Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the one who believed in it is Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام)." This is what is narrated from the Imams of guidance from the Progeny of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Hurayrah. It is apparent that this is an instance of "running" (الجري), considering His saying at the end of the verse: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُتَّقُونَ — it is they who are the God-conscious.

It was narrated through their paths that the one who believed in it was Abu Bakr, which is also an application by the narrator. It was also narrated that the one who brought it was Gabriel and the one who believed in it was Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); this too is an application, but the context repels it, for the verses are set forth to describe the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers, and Gabriel is foreign to this, having no connection to the discourse.

Ayahs

وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلْ أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَادَنِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ هَلْ هُنَّ كَـٰشِفَـٰتُ ضُرِّهِۦٓ أَوْ أَرَادَنِى بِرَحْمَةٍ هَلْ هُنَّ مُمْسِكَـٰتُ رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ قُلْ حَسْبِىَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ عَلَيْهِ يَتَوَكَّلُ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ (٣٨)
If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘Have you considered what you invoke besides Allah? Should Allah desire some distress for me, can they remove the distress visited by Him? Or should He desire some mercy for me, can they withhold His mercy?’ Say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me. In Him let all the trusting put their trust.’ (38)
قُلْ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنِّى عَـٰمِلٌ ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٩)
Say, ‘O my people! Act according to your ability. I too am acting. Soon you will know (39)
مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٤٠)
who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him, and on whom a lasting punishment will descend.’ (40)
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لِلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ (٤١)
Indeed We have sent down the Book to you with the truth for [the deliverance of] mankind. So whoever is guided is guided for his own sake, and whoever goes astray, goes astray to his own detriment, and it is not your duty to watch over them. (41)
ٱللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى ٱلْأَنفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِهَا وَٱلَّتِى لَمْ تَمُتْ فِى مَنَامِهَا ۖ فَيُمْسِكُ ٱلَّتِى قَضَىٰ عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَوْتَ وَيُرْسِلُ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٤٢)
Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those who have not died, in their sleep. Then He retains those for whom He has ordained death and releases the others until a specified time. There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect. (42)
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ شُفَعَآءَ ۚ قُلْ أَوَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٤٣)
Have they taken intercessors besides Allah? Say, ‘What! Even though they do not control anything, and cannot reason?!’ (43)
قُل لِّلَّهِ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةُ جَمِيعًا ۖ لَّهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٤٤)
Say, ‘All intercession rests with Allah. To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth; then you will be brought back to Him.’ (44)
وَإِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ ٱشْمَأَزَّتْ قُلُوبُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَإِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِذَا هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (٤٥)
When Allah is mentioned alone, [thereat] shrink away the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter, but when others are mentioned besides Him, behold, they rejoice! (45)
قُلِ ٱللَّهُمَّ فَاطِرَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ عَـٰلِمَ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ أَنتَ تَحْكُمُ بَيْنَ عِبَادِكَ فِى مَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (٤٦)
Say, ‘O Allah! Originator of the heavens and the earth, Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, You will judge between Your servants concerning that about which they used to differ.’ (46)
وَلَوْ أَنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَمِثْلَهُۥ مَعَهُۥ لَٱفْتَدَوْا۟ بِهِۦ مِن سُوٓءِ ٱلْعَذَابِ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ وَبَدَا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ يَحْتَسِبُونَ (٤٧)
Even if the wrongdoers possessed all that is on the earth, and as much of it besides, they would offer it on the Day of Resurrection to redeem themselves with it from a terrible punishment, and there will appear to them from Allah what they had never reckoned. (47)
وَبَدَا لَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا كَسَبُوا۟ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٤٨)
The evils of what they had earned will appear to them, and they will be besieged by what they used to deride. (48)
فَإِذَا مَسَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ ضُرٌّ دَعَانَا ثُمَّ إِذَا خَوَّلْنَـٰهُ نِعْمَةً مِّنَّا قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أُوتِيتُهُۥ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍۭ ۚ بَلْ هِىَ فِتْنَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٩)
When distress befalls man, he supplicates Us. Then, when We grant him a blessing from Us, he says, ‘I was given it by virtue of [my] knowledge.’ Indeed, it is a test, but most of them do not know. (49)
قَدْ قَالَهَا ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٥٠)
Those who were before them [also] said that, but what they used to earn did not avail them. (50)
فَأَصَابَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا كَسَبُوا۟ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ سَيُصِيبُهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا كَسَبُوا۟ وَمَا هُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (٥١)
So the evils of what they had earned visited them, and as for the wrongdoers among these, the evils of what they earn shall be visited on them and they will not frustrate [Allah]. (51)
أَوَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٢)
Do they not know that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes and tightens it [for whomever He wishes]? There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (52)

(Commentary)

In these verses, there is another turn against the polytheists (المشركين) by arguing for His uniqueness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية) — the absolute authority of Allah in creating, sustaining, and governing all existence — and that their partners are not fit for it. It asserts that the intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — which they claim for their partners is owned by none but Allah, the Glorified. The verses also contain other matters related to the call (الدعوة), such as exhortation, warning, and glad tidings.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ "If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’" (39:38) until the end of the verse, is a commencement in establishing the argument. He has prefaced it with a premise upon which the argument is built, which is conceded by the opponent: that the Creator of the world is Allah, the Glorified. Indeed, the opponent does not dispute that the Creator is Allah alone, without any partner; they only claim for their partners the role of governance (التدبير) rather than creation (الخلق).

Since creation belongs to Him, the Exalted, then whatever exists in the heavens and the earth, whether an essence (عين) or an effect (أثر), its existence ultimately traces back to Him, the Exalted. Thus, whatever good or evil befalls anything, its existence is from Him, the Exalted. No one has the power to withhold a good He intends for it, nor to remove an evil He intends for it, because this pertains to creation (الخلق) and origination (الإيجاد), and He, the Exalted, has no partner in creation and origination such that anyone could compete with Him in creating something, prevent Him from creating something, or precede Him in creating something. Governance (التدبير) — the orderly arrangement of affairs and the sequential organization of one thing upon another — is itself creation and origination. Thus, Allah, the Creator of everything, is sufficient in governing the affairs of the world because He is the Creator of everything. There is nothing beyond creation such that it could be imagined to depend on other than Him. He is Allah, the Lord of everything and its God; there is no Lord besides Him and no God other than Him.

Thus, His saying: قُلْ أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ "Say, ‘Tell me, then, about those you invoke besides Allah.’" (39:38) means: establish the argument against them, basing it on this premise conceded by them—that Allah is the Creator of everything—and say, branching from it: "Inform me about those you invoke besides Allah." The use of the word "what" (ما) to refer to their deities instead of "who" (من) or similar terms serves to generalize the explanation to include both the idols and their lords (أربابها) together. While the elite among them might restrict worship to the lords among the angels and others, taking the idols only as a direction (قبلة) and a means (ذريعة) to turn toward those lords, their common folk might take the idols themselves as lords and gods to be worshipped. The result of the argument is general, encompassing all of them.

And His saying: إِنْ أَرادَنِيَ اللهُ بِضُرٍّ هَلْ هُنَّ كاشِفاتُ ضُرِّهِ أَوْ أَرادَنِي بِرَحْمَةٍ هَلْ هُنَّ مُمْسِكاتُ رَحْمَتِهِ "If Allah desired to cause me any distress, can they remove His distress? Or if He desired to grant me any mercy, can they withhold His mercy?" (39:38). Distress (الضر) refers to things like illness and hardship, and its apparent contrast with mercy (الرحمة) indicates its generality for every calamity. The attribution of distress and mercy to His pronoun, the Exalted, in "His distress" (ضُرِّهِ) and "His mercy" (رَحْمَتِهِ) is to preserve the relationship, because what prevents the removal of distress or the withholding of mercy is their attribution to Him, the Exalted.

The specific mention of distress and mercy regarding him (the Prophet), صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, despite the generality of the argument for him and others, is because he is the primary antagonist to them, and they had tried to frighten him with their deities besides Allah.

The return of the feminine plural pronoun (هنّ) to "what they invoke besides Allah" is to give preponderance to the non-rational side, namely the idols. This supports what we previously mentioned regarding His saying: أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ "Tell me, then, about those you invoke besides Allah," that the expression "what" (ما) is used to generalize the argument to both the idols and their lords.

And His saying: قُلْ حَسْبِيَ اللهُ "Say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me.’" (39:38) is a command to place trust (التوكل) — the act of relying solely upon Allah while acknowledging Him as the ultimate Disposer of affairs — in Him, the Exalted, as indicated by His subsequent saying: عَلَيْهِ يَتَوَكَّلُ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ "In Him let all the trusting put their trust." (39:38). This is placed in the position of the conclusion of the argument, as if it were said: "Say to them: I have taken Allah as a guardian (وكيلاً) because the matter of my governance belongs to Him just as the matter of my creation belongs to Him." It carries the meaning of our saying: "The argument has demonstrated His Lordship, and I have confirmed that in practice by taking Him as a guardian in my affairs."

And His saying: عَلَيْهِ يَتَوَكَّلُ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ "In Him let all the trusting put their trust" (39:38). The advancement of the prepositional phrase (عَلَيْهِ) before its related verb is to indicate exclusivity (الحصر), meaning: in Him they trust, and not in anyone else. The attribution of the verb to the description derived from its root (الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ) is to indicate that what is meant are those who trust according to the true meaning of trust. Thus, the sentence contains praise for Him, the Exalted, as being the One worthy of trust; in Him do the people of insight (أهل البصيرة) in trust place their reliance. Therefore, there is no blame upon me if I trust in Him and say: "Allah is sufficient for me."

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يا قَوْمِ اعْمَلُوا عَلى مَكانَتِكُمْ إِنِّي عامِلٌ "Say, ‘O my people! Act according to your ability; I too am acting.’" (39:39) until His saying: عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ "an everlasting punishment." (39:40). Position (المكانة) refers to status and capacity; it is in the realm of the intelligible (المعقولات) like place (المكان) is in the realm of the sensible (المحسوسات). Thus, commanding them to act according to their position means commanding them to continue in the state they are in—of disbelief (الكفر), stubbornness (العناد), and barring others from the way of Allah.

And His saying: فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ يَأْتِيهِ عَذابٌ يُخْزِيهِ "Soon you will know who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him" (39:39). It appears that "who" (مَنْ) is interrogative (استفهامية) rather than a relative pronoun (موصولة), because knowledge (العلم) clearly relates here to the sentence as a whole rather than a single noun.

And His saying: وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ "and on whom will descend an everlasting punishment." (39:40). That is, a permanent one, which is appropriate for the term "descending" (الحلول). The separation of the two punishments suggests that what is meant by the first is the punishment of this world, and by the second, the punishment of the Hereafter. The speech contains the severest of threats.

The meaning is: Say, addressing the polytheists of your people: "O my people! Act—continuing—in your state of disbelief and stubbornness; I too am acting—as I am commanded, without turning away from it—and soon you will know who will be overtaken by a punishment that disgraces and humbles him." This is the punishment of this world, as occurred on the day of Badr. "And on whom will descend and never leave him a permanent punishment," which is the punishment of the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ لِلنَّاسِ بِالْحَقِ "Indeed We have sent down to you the Book with the truth for [the sake of] mankind." (39:41) until the end of the verse. This is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the command in the previous verse. The "L" (لـ) in His saying "for mankind" (لِلنَّاسِ) is for causation, meaning: for the sake of mankind, that you may recite it to them and convey to them what is in it. The "B" (بـ) in His saying "with the truth" (بِالْحَقِ) is for accompaniment (الملابسة), meaning: accompanied by the truth, unmixed with any falsehood.

And His saying: فَمَنِ اهْتَدى فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ ضَلَّ فَإِنَّما يَضِلُّ عَلَيْها "So whoever is guided, it is for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, he goes astray only to its detriment." (39:41). That is, it follows from this sending down that whoever is guided, the benefit of the happiness of life and the reward of the Abode of the Hereafter returns only to his own soul. And whoever goes astray and is not guided by it, the misery and evil consequence of the punishment of the Abode of the Hereafter returns only to his own soul. Allah, the Glorified, is far too exalted to benefit from their guidance or be harmed by their straying.

And His saying: وَما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِوَكِيلٍ "And you are not a guardian over them." (39:41). That is, you are not one to whom their affair has been delegated, standing in charge of governing their affairs such that you must deliver the guidance within it to their hearts.

The meaning is: We only commanded you to threaten them with what We said because We sent down the Book to you with the truth for the sake of reading it to the people and nothing else. So whoever among them is guided, the benefit returns to his own soul, and whoever goes astray and is not guided by it, the harm returns to his own soul. You are not a guardian from Us over them, governing their affairs to deliver guidance to their hearts; you have no part in the matter.

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death" (39:42) until the end of the verse. It is said in al-Majma': Taking (التوفي) is to receive a thing in full and complete (على الإيفاء والإتمام). It is said: "I took (توفيت) my right from so-and-so" and "I received it in full (استوفيته)," with the same meaning. End quote.

The advancement of the subject (المسند إليه) in the verse indicates exclusivity (الحصر), meaning: He, the Exalted, is the One who takes them, and no other. When this verse is joined with others like His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ "Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls’" (32:11), and His saying: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا "until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him" (6:61), it conveys the meaning of primary agency (الأصالة) and subordinate agency (التبعية). That is, He, the Exalted, is the One who takes the souls in reality, while the Angel of Death and the angels who are his assistants are intermediary causes (أسباب متوسطة) acting by His command.

And His saying: اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death." What is meant by souls (الأنفس) are the spirits (الأرواح) attached to the bodies, not the composite of spirits and bodies, because the composite is not "taken" at death; rather, what is taken is the spirit. It is taken from the body in the sense of severing its attachment to the body—the attachment of disposal and governance. What is meant by "their death" is the death of their bodies, either by assuming a suppressed genitive (مضاف) or through a form of intellectual metaphor (مجاز عقلي). The same applies to what is meant by "their sleep."

And His saying: وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنامِها "and those that have not died, in their sleep" (39:42) is coordinated with "the souls" in the previous sentence. It appears that "sleep" (المنام) is a noun of time, and "in their sleep" is related to "takes" (يتوفى). The implied meaning is: "and He takes the souls that have not died at the time of their sleep."

Then He, the Exalted, details the state of the souls taken at the time of sleep, saying: فَيُمْسِكُ الَّتِي قَضى عَلَيْهَا الْمَوْتَ وَيُرْسِلُ الْأُخْرى إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى "Then He retains those for whom He has decided death and releases the others until a specified time." (39:42). That is, He keeps the soul for which He has decreed death, just as He keeps the soul He took at the time of its death, and does not return it to its body. And He releases the other soul, for which He has not decreed death, back to its body until a specified term (أجل مسمى) — the fixed and predetermined lifespan of an individual — at which life ends.

Making the specified term the end-point for the "releasing" is evidence that what is meant by releasing is the genus of the act, meaning that He releases some souls once, and some time after time, until they reach the specified term.

From the verse, it is inferred, firstly: that the soul (النفس) is an existent distinct from the body, such that it can separate from it, become independent of it, and remain on its own.

Secondly: that death and sleep are both a "taking" (توفٍ), even if they differ in that death is a taking after which there is no releasing, while sleep is a taking after which there may be a releasing.

Then He completed the verse by saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ "There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect." (39:42). Thus, they remember that Allah, the Glorified, is the Governor of their affairs and that to Him they shall return; He will call them to account for what they have done.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ شُفَعاءَ "Have they taken intercessors besides Allah?" (39:43) etc. The word "or" (أَمِ) is disjunctive (منقطعة), meaning: "Nay, the polytheists have taken intercessors besides Allah," and these are their deities whom they worship so that they may intercede for them with Allah, the Glorified, as He said at the beginning of the Surah: ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللهِ زُلْفى "We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah" (39:3), and He said: يَقُولُونَ هؤُلاءِ شُفَعاؤُنا عِنْدَ اللهِ "They say, ‘These are our intercessors with Allah.’" (10:18).

And His saying: قُلْ أَوَلَوْ كانُوا لا يَمْلِكُونَ شَيْئاً وَلا يَعْقِلُونَ "Say, ‘What! Even if they do not control anything and do not understand?’" (39:43). He commanded him to refute them by challenging the absolute nature of their claim. It is self-evident that intercession depends on knowledge in the intercessor, by which he knows what he wants, from whom he wants it, and for whom he wants it. Thus, there is no meaning to the intercession of an inanimate object (الجماد) that has no consciousness. Likewise, it depends on the intercessor owning the intercession and having the right to intercede. No one possesses ownership except Allah, unless Allah grants someone ownership of something and permits them to act within it. Their claim of intercession for their "guardians" (أوليائهم) in an absolute sense—including those who own nothing and of whose permission from Allah they have no knowledge—is mere conjecture (تخرص).

Thus, the interrogation in "What! Even if they..." etc., is for denial (الإنكار). The meaning is: Say to them, "Do you take them as intercessors for you even if they possess nothing of themselves, like the angels, and understand nothing, like the idols?" For that is foolishness.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِلَّهِ الشَّفاعَةُ جَمِيعاً لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ "Say, ‘All intercession belongs to Allah. To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth.’" (39:44) etc. This is an explanation and confirmation of what preceded in His saying: "Say, ‘What! Even if they do not control anything.’" The "L" (لـ) in "belongs to Allah" (لِلَّهِ) is for ownership (الملك). His saying: "To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth" is in the position of providing the reason for the previous sentence. The meaning is: every intercession is owned by Allah, for He is the Owner of everything, unless He permits someone in some part of it, thereby granting them ownership of it. As for some of His servants, like the angels, independently possessing the right of intercession absolutely as they claim, that is something that cannot be. He, the Exalted, said: ما مِنْ شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ "There is no intercessor, except after His leave." (10:3).

The verse has another, more subtle meaning when joined with verses like His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ وَليٌّ وَلا شَفِيعٌ "they have no guardian or intercessor besides Him" (6:51). This is that the Intercessor in reality is Allah, the Glorified, and other intercessors have intercession by permission from Him. It has already been mentioned in the discussion on intercession in the first volume of this book that intercession ultimately traces back to the mediation of some of His attributes, the Exalted, between Him and the one interceded for, to rectify his state—such as the mediation of mercy (الرحمة) and forgiveness (المغفرة) between Him and His sinful servant to save him from the evil consequence of sin and deliver him from punishment.

The difference between this ownership and what was in the previous view is that the owner is not described by his property in the previous view, as in Zayd's ownership of a house. This is in contrast to ownership in this view, where the owner is described by his property, like the ownership of a brave man of his bravery.

And His saying: ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ "then to Him you will be brought back." (39:44). This is another reason for His owning all intercession, which indicates exclusivity. This is because intercession is only owned by the one to whom the affair of the one interceded for ultimately returns; if He wills, He accepts it and rectifies the state of the one interceded for. As for anyone else, they only own it if He is pleased with it and permits it. Allah, the Glorified, is the One to whom the servants return, not those whom they invoke besides Allah. Thus, Allah is the Owner of all intercession. Their claim that their guardians are intercessors for them absolutely, and then their worship of them based on that, is a structure without a foundation to rely upon.

It is said: His saying: "then to Him you will be brought back" is a threat to them, as if it were said: "Then to Him you will return, and you will know that they do not intercede for you, and your effort in worshipping them will be frustrated."

It is also said: It is possible that it is an explicit statement of the ownership of the Hereafter, wherein lies the greatest benefit of intercession, and an allusion to the cessation of formal ownership (الملك الصوري) from everything besides Him, the Exalted. The correct view is what we previously stated.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا ذُكِرَ اللهُ وَحْدَهُ اشْمَأَزَّتْ قُلُوبُ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ "When Allah is mentioned alone, the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter shrink with aversion" (39:45) etc. What is meant by mentioning Him, the Exalted, alone is making Him the sole object of mention without mentioning their deities; among its instances is the saying "There is no god but Allah." Aversion (الاشمئزاز) is the contraction and recoiling from a thing.

He only mentioned the description of their lack of belief in the Hereafter because that is the root of their aversion. Had they been believers in the Hereafter and that they would return to Allah so He might recompense them for their deeds, they would have worshipped Him to the exclusion of their guardians and would not have turned away from mentioning Him alone.

And His saying: وَإِذا ذُكِرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِذا هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ "but when those besides Him are mentioned, behold, they rejoice." (39:45). What is meant by "those besides Him" are their deities. Rejoicing (الاستبشار) is the joy of the heart such that its effect appears on the face.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللهُمَّ فاطِرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ عالِمَ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ أَنْتَ تَحْكُمُ "Say, ‘O Allah! Originator of the heavens and the earth, Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, You will judge...’" (39:46) etc. When the discourse reached a point where no good could be hoped for from them due to their forgetting the matter of the Hereafter and their denial of the return to Him, the Exalted—to the extent that they would recoil from the mention of Allah alone—He commanded him, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, to mention Allah alone and to remind them of His judgment between His servants in that which they differed, in the form of seeking refuge in Him, the Exalted, along with what it contains of the affirmation of the Resurrection (البعث). He described Allah, the Exalted, as the Originator (فاطر) of the heavens and the earth—meaning the One who brings them out from the concealment of non-existence into the arena of existence—and the Knower of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — and the Manifest (الشهادة), so nothing is hidden from Him. This necessitates that He judges with truth and that His judgment is executed.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً وَمِثْلَهُ مَعَهُ لَافْتَدَوْا بِهِ مِنْ سُوءِ الْعَذابِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ "Even if the wrongdoers possessed all that is on the earth, and as much of it besides, they would surely offer it as ransom from the terrible punishment on the Day of Resurrection." (39:47) etc. What is meant by "those who do wrong" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا) are those who did wrong in this world; the verb functions as a description. The wrongdoers (الظالمون) are those who deny the Return (المعاد), as He said: أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ كافِرُونَ "The curse of Allah is on the wrongdoers, who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter." (7:44-45).

The meaning is: Even if the wrongdoers who deny the Return possessed twice what is on the earth of wealth, stores, and treasures, they would surely offer it as a ransom from the evil punishment.

And His saying: وَبَدا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ ما لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ "and there will appear to them from Allah what they had never reckoned." (39:47). Appearance (البداء) and appearing (البدو) mean manifestation. Calculation (الحساب) and reckoning (الحسبان) mean counting. To reckon (الاحتساب) is to take something into account in the sense of building upon its being something; often, reckoning and calculation are used in the sense of "supposing" (الظن), as has been said, and from this is His saying: "what they had never reckoned," meaning what they had never supposed. However, al-Raghib distinguished between reckoning and supposition, saying: "Reckoning is to judge in favor of one of two opposites without the other even crossing one's mind, while being subject to doubt therein. Supposition is close to that, but supposition is when both opposites cross one's mind and one prevails over the other." End quote.

The context of the verse necessitates that what is meant is to explain that they will face on the Day of Resurrection matters of a nature that is beyond what they imagined, and greater and more horrific than what crossed their minds—not that they will witness things they did not believe in or acknowledge. In short, they used to hear that Allah has a reckoning, a weighing of deeds, a judgment, a Fire, and various types of punishment, so they would measure what they heard—while denying it—against what they were familiar with of these matters in this world. When they witness them as they appear to them, they will find them greater than what used to cross their minds regarding their description. This verse, in describing His punishment, is similar to His saying in describing the bliss of the people of Paradise: فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ "No soul knows what has been kept hidden for them of the joy of their eyes" (32:17).

Also, the context necessitates that the "appearing" mentioned is of the nature of manifestation after concealment and disclosure after being hidden, as alluded to by His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today." (50:22).

His saying, the Exalted: وَبَدا لَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا "The evils of what they had earned will appear to them" (39:48) until the end of the verse. That is, the evils of their deeds will appear to them after they were hidden from them. This is like His saying: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done..." (3:30).

And His saying: وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ "and they will be besieged by what they used to deride." (39:48). That is, there shall descend upon them and strike them that which they used to mock in the world when they heard it from the guardians of the religion—the hardships of the Day of Resurrection, its horrors, and its types of punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ ضُرٌّ دَعانا ثُمَّ إِذا خَوَّلْناهُ نِعْمَةً مِنَّا قالَ إِنَّما أُوتِيتُهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ "When distress befalls man, he invokes Us. Then, when We grant him a blessing from Us, he says, ‘I was given it only because of [my] knowledge.’" (39:49) etc. The verse is in the position of an explanatory reason (التعليل البياني) for the preceding description of the wrongdoers. Therefore, it began with the "F" (فـ) to branch from what preceded as an explanation branches from the explained.

Since He, the Exalted, mentioned of their state that they turned away from every sign indicating the truth, did not listen to the arguments established against them, did not hear any exhortation, and did not take heed of any lesson—thus rejecting His Lordship, the Exalted, and denying the Resurrection and the Reckoning, reaching the point where their hearts recoiled when Allah alone was mentioned—He explained that this is something called for by the nature of man (طبع الإنسان), which is inclined toward following the desires of his soul and being deceived by the blessings of the world and the apparent causes surrounding them. Man is an ally of forgetfulness; when distress touches him, he turns to his Lord, becomes sincere to Him, and invokes Him. Then, when his Lord grants him a blessing, he attributes it to his own knowledge and expertise, forgets his Lord, and is ignorant that it is a trial (فتنة) by which he is being tested.

So His saying: فَإِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ ضُرٌّ "When distress befalls man," meaning illness or hardship, دَعانا "he invokes Us," meaning he singles Us out for invocation and cuts himself off from others.

And His saying: ثُمَّ إِذا خَوَّلْناهُ نِعْمَةً مِنَّا قالَ إِنَّما أُوتِيتُهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ "Then, when We grant him a blessing from Us, he says, ‘I was given it only because of [my] knowledge.’" Granting (التخويل) is giving in the manner of a gift. Restricting the blessing with the phrase "from Us" (مِنَّا) is to indicate that the description of it being a blessing is preserved for it. The meaning is: We granted him what is clearly a blessing.

The pronoun in "I was given it" (أُوتِيتُهُ) refers to the blessing insofar as it is a thing or wealth. The intent in this is to point out that he does not acknowledge it as being a blessing from Us; rather, he severs it from Us, calling it a "thing" or "wealth" or similar, and does not call it a "blessing" so that he would not be forced to acknowledge a Bestower (منعم) and point to Him. As he said: "I was given it," he ignored the Agent of that. These two expressions—"a blessing from Us" and "I was given it"—are among the subtle expressions of the Quran. Others have explained the masculinity of the pronoun in "I was given it" in other ways that are not well-founded; whoever desires them may refer to the detailed commentaries.

What is consistent with the context of the verse is that the meaning of "because of knowledge" (عَلى عِلْمٍ) is "because of knowledge from me," meaning: "I was given this which I was given because of knowledge from me and expertise in the ways of earning a living, acquiring wealth, and amassing money."

It is said: The meaning is "I was given it because of knowledge from Allah of a good in me by which I deserve that He give me the blessing." It is also said: The meaning is "because of knowledge from me of Allah's pleasure with me." You are aware that what preceded regarding the meaning of His saying: "Then, when We grant him a blessing from Us, he says, ‘I was given it...’" does not fit either of these two sayings.

And His saying: بَلْ هِيَ فِتْنَةٌ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "No, it is a trial, but most of them do not know." (39:49). That is, rather, the blessing We granted him from Us is a trial (فتنة) — a test and examination by which we try him — but most of them do not know that.

It is said: Its meaning is "rather, that blessing is a punishment for them." It is also said: The meaning is "rather, this statement is a trial for them for which they will be punished." Both views are far-fetched, especially the latter.

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ قالَهَا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَما أَغْنى عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ فَأَصابَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا "Those who were before them [also] said it, but what they used to earn did not avail them. So the evils of what they had earned overtook them." (39:50-51). The pronoun in "said it" (قالَهَا) refers to the previous saying, considering it a statement or a word.

The verse is a rebuttal of their saying and a proof that it is a trial by which they are tested. If they had been given it because of knowledge from them and had earned it by their own power and strength, their earning would have availed them, the evils of what they earned would not have overtaken them, they would have preserved it for themselves and enjoyed it, and they would not have perished despite it. But it is not so; those before them said this statement, yet their earning did not avail them, and the evils of what they earned overtook them.

It appears that the verse, in saying "Those who were before them [also] said it," refers to Qarun and his likes. The saying إِنَّما أُوتِيتُهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي "I have indeed been given it because of the knowledge I have" (28:78) has been narrated from him in his story in Surah al-Qasas.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْ هؤُلاءِ سَيُصِيبُهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا وَما هُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ "As for those who are wrongdoers among these, the evils of what they have earned will overtake them [too], and they will not frustrate [Allah]." (39:51). The reference "these" (هؤُلاءِ) is to the people of him (the Prophet), صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The meaning is that those who have done wrong among your people, their path is the path of those before them; the evils of their earning and the consequences of their deeds will overtake them, and they cannot frustrate Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَقْدِرُ "Do they not know that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it?" (39:52) etc. This is another answer to the saying of the one among them who said: "I was given it only because of [my] knowledge." The first answer, "Those who were before them [also] said it," etc., was an answer by way of counter-example (النقض), while this is an answer by way of counter-argument (المعارضة) by pointing to the evidence that Allah, the Glorified, is the One who expands provision and restricts it.

The explanation of this is: Man's effort based on knowledge and will to acquire provision (الرزق) is not a complete cause (سبب تام) that necessitates the attainment of provision; otherwise, it would never fail, yet the opposite is clearly evident. How many a seeker has returned in despair, and how many a striver's effort has failed!

There are causes, temporal and spatial conditions, and various obstacles differing with circumstances, which are beyond counting. When these gather and coincide, they result in the attainment of provision.

The gathering of these causes and conditions—despite their variation, dispersion, and separation in terms of matter, time, place, and other related requirements, whether concurrent or preceding, and the causes of causes and their preliminaries that go on infinitely—is not a gathering and coincidence by way of chance (الاتفاق). For chance is neither permanent nor frequent. The law of the provision of the provided-for, which encompasses living beings and indeed extends over the regions and horizons of the witnessed world, is fixed and preserved in a system (نظام) that runs according to its breadth and expansion. Had it been severed, things would have perished in the first moment and instantly.

This running system, with its unity and the proportion and harmony of its parts, reveals the oneness of its Organizer and the uniqueness of its Governor and Manager, who is outside the parts of the world that are preserved by the system itself and remain through it—and He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

Furthermore, the system is part of governance (التدبير), and governance is part of creation (الخلق), as has been mentioned repeatedly. Thus, the Creator of the world is its Governor, and its Governor is its Provider, and He is Allah, exalted is His state.

This proof is alluded to in the verse by His saying: لِمَنْ يَشاءُ "for whomever He wishes." For if the expansion of provision and its restriction are by His will, the Exalted, then it is not by the will of the human who boasts of his knowledge and effort, nor by the will of any of the causes and means and their necessity, as is apparent. It is not of the nature of chance; rather, it is according to a running system. Thus, it is by the will of the Maker of the system and its Executor, and He is Allah, the Glorified.

A discussion on the meaning of provision (الرزق) has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرْزُقُ مَنْ تَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ "and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning" (3:27), and a discussion on it will follow in the exegesis of His saying: فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِثْلَ ما أَنَّكُمْ تَنْطِقُونَ "By the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is indeed as real as your speaking [to one another]!" (51:23), if Allah wills.

( Hadith Discussion )

In al-Tawhid, narrated from Imam Ali (peace be upon him) in a long tradition: A man asked him about verses that seemed ambiguous to him. He (peace be upon him) said: وأما قوله : «يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ» وقوله : «اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها» وقوله : «تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا وَهُمْ لا يُفَرِّطُونَ» وقوله : «الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ» وقوله : «الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ يَقُولُونَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ» فإن الله تبارك وتعالى يدبر الأمر كيف يشاء ـ ويوكل من خلقه من يشاء بما يشاء ـ أما ملك الموت ـ فإن الله يوكله بخاصته ممن يشاء من خلقه ـ ويوكل رسله من الملائكة خاصة بمن يشاء من خلقه ـ. وليس كل العلم يستطيع صاحب العلم ـ أن يفسره لكل الناس ـ لأن فيهم القوي والضعيف ، ولأن منه ما يطاق حمله ومنه ما لا يطاق حمله ـ إلا أن يسهل الله له حمله ـ وأعانه عليه من خاصة أوليائه. وإنما يكفيك أن تعلم أن الله المحيي المميت ، وأنه يتوفى الأنفس على يدي من يشاء من خلقه ـ من ملائكته وغيرهم. — "As for His saying: 'The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls' (32:11), and His saying: 'Allah takes the souls at the time of their death' (39:42), and His saying: 'Our messengers take him, and they do not fail' (6:61), and His saying: 'those whom the angels take while they are wronging themselves' (16:28), and His saying: 'those whom the angels take while they are good, saying, "Peace be upon you"' (16:32)—indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, governs the matter as He wills, and He charges whom He wills of His creation with what He wills. As for the Angel of Death, Allah charges him with the elite of whom He wills of His creation, and He charges His messengers from among the angels specifically with whom He wills of His creation. Not all knowledge can the possessor of knowledge explain to all people, for among them are the strong and the weak, and because some of it can be borne and some cannot be borne—unless Allah makes its bearing easy for him and aids him through the elite of His friends. It is sufficient for you to know that Allah is the Giver of life and the Giver of death, and that He takes the souls by the hands of whom He wills of His creation—from His angels and others."

In al-Khisal, narrated from Imam Ali (peace be upon him) in the "Four Hundred Hadith": لا ينام المسلم وهو جنب لا ينام إلا على طهور ـ فإن لم يجد الماء فليتيمم بالصعيد ـ فإن روح المؤمن ترفع إلى الله تعالى ـ فيقبلها ويبارك عليها ـ فإن كان أجلها قد حضر جعلها في كنوز رحمته ـ وإن لم يكن أجلها قد حضر ـ بعث بها مع أمنائه من ملائكته فيردونها في جسده. — "A Muslim should not sleep while in a state of ritual impurity (جنب); he should not sleep except in a state of purity (طهور). If he does not find water, let him perform dry ablution (تيمم) with clean earth. For the spirit of the believer is raised to Allah, the Exalted; He accepts it and blesses it. If its term has arrived, He places it among the treasures of His mercy. If its term has not yet arrived, He sends it back with the trustworthy ones among His angels, and they return it to its body."

In al-Majma', al-Ayyashi narrated with a chain of transmission from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Amr ibn Thabit, from Abu al-Miqdam, from his father, from Imam Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: ما من أحد ينام إلا عرجت نفسه إلى السماء ـ وبقيت روحه في بدنه ـ وصار بينهما سبب كشعاع الشمس ـ فإن أذن الله في قبض الأرواح أجابت الروح النفس ـ وإن أذن الله في رد الروح أجابت النفس الروح ـ وهو قوله سبحانه : «اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها» الآية ـ. فمهما رأت في ملكوت السموات فهو مما له تأويل ـ وما رأت فيما بين السماء والأرض ـ فهو مما يخيله الشيطان ولا تأويل له. — "There is no one who sleeps but that his soul (نفسه) ascends to the heaven, while his spirit (روحه) remains in his body, and there becomes between them a connection like a ray of the sun. If Allah permits the seizing of the spirits, the spirit answers the soul; and if Allah permits the return of the spirit, the soul answers the spirit. This is His saying, the Glorified: 'Allah takes the souls at the time of their death' (39:42). Whatever it sees in the kingdom of the heavens (ملكوت السموات) is of that which has an interpretation (تأويل), and whatever it sees between the heaven and the earth is of that which the Shaytan imagines for it, and it has no interpretation."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Sulaym ibn Amir that Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "It is a wonder regarding a man's dream—that he spends the night and sees something that never crossed his mind, and his dream is like something held in the hand; yet a man sees a dream and his dream is nothing."

Ali ibn Abi Talib said: أفلا أخبرك بذلك يا أمير المؤمنين يقول الله تعالى : «اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنامِهاـفَيُمْسِكُ الَّتِي قَضى عَلَيْهَا الْمَوْتَـوَيُرْسِلُ الْأُخْرى إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى» فالله يتوفى الأنفس كلها ـ فما رأت وهي عنده في السماء فهي الرؤيا الصادقة ، وما رأت إذا أرسلت إلى أجسادها ـ تلقيها الشياطين في الهواء ـ فكذبتها وأخبرتها بالأباطيل — "Shall I not inform you of that, O Commander of the Faithful? Allah, the Exalted, says: 'Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those that have not died, in their sleep. Then He retains those for whom He has decided death and releases the others until a specified time' (39:42). Thus, Allah takes all the souls. Whatever it sees while it is with Him in the heaven is the true vision (الرؤيا الصادقة), and whatever it sees when it is released to its body—the devils encounter it in the air, lying to it and informing it of falsehoods." Umar was amazed by his saying.

I say: The detailed discussion on dreams (الرؤيا) has already preceded in Surah Yusuf, and referring back to it helps in understanding the meaning of these two narrations. In them, "the heaven" (السماء) has been applied to what is technically termed the Greater Imaginal World (عالم المثال الأعظم), and "between the heaven and the earth" to what is technically termed the Lesser Imaginal World (عالم المثال الأصغر). So observe.

Ayahs

۞ قُلْ يَـٰعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَا تَقْنَطُوا۟ مِن رَّحْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥٣)
Say [that Allah declares,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah will forgive all sins. Indeed, He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. (53)
وَأَنِيبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ وَأَسْلِمُوا۟ لَهُۥ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ (٥٤)
Turn penitently to Him and submit to Him before the punishment overtakes you, whereupon you will not be helped. (54)
وَٱتَّبِعُوٓا۟ أَحْسَنَ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ بَغْتَةً وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ (٥٥)
Follow the best of what has been sent down to you from your Lord, before the punishment overtakes you suddenly while you are unaware.’ (55)
أَن تَقُولَ نَفْسٌ يَـٰحَسْرَتَىٰ عَلَىٰ مَا فَرَّطتُ فِى جَنۢبِ ٱللَّهِ وَإِن كُنتُ لَمِنَ ٱلسَّـٰخِرِينَ (٥٦)
Lest anyone should say, ‘Alas for my negligence in the vicinage of Allah! Indeed I was among those who ridiculed.’ (56)
أَوْ تَقُولَ لَوْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هَدَىٰنِى لَكُنتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٥٧)
Or say, ‘Had Allah guided me I would have surely been among the Godwary!’ (57)
أَوْ تَقُولَ حِينَ تَرَى ٱلْعَذَابَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِى كَرَّةً فَأَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٨)
Or say, when he sights the punishment, ‘If only there were a second chance for me, I would be among the virtuous!’ (58)
بَلَىٰ قَدْ جَآءَتْكَ ءَايَـٰتِى فَكَذَّبْتَ بِهَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرْتَ وَكُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٩)
