[They will be told,] ‘Yes, My signs certainly came to you, but you denied them and acted arrogantly and you were among the faithless.’ (59)
وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ تَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وُجُوهُهُم مُّسْوَدَّةٌ ۚ أَلَيْسَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوًى لِّلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٦٠)
On the Day of Resurrection you will see those who attributed lies to Allah with their faces blackened. Is not the [final] abode of the arrogant in hell? (60)
وَيُنَجِّى ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ بِمَفَازَتِهِمْ لَا يَمَسُّهُمُ ٱلسُّوٓءُ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦١)
Allah will deliver those who were Godwary with their salvation. No ill shall touch them, nor will they grieve. (61)

(Commentary)

In these verses, He commands him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to invite them to Islam and to follow what Allah has sent down, and to warn them of the consequence of their extravagance (إسراف) against their own souls—namely, the regret and remorse on a Day when such feelings will not benefit them, given their arrogance (استكبار) toward the Truth in the world. Success and salvation on that Day are for the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness (تقوى), the inner shield of protection against Allah's displeasure through the fulfillment of duties and avoidance of sins — while the Fire and loss are for the disbelievers (الكافرين). Within the tone of these verses, there is a manifest tenderness and mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ "Say, [O Prophet, that Allah says,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah...’" (39:53). He commands him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to call them on His behalf and to address them with the expression "O My servants" (يا عِبادِيَ). In this, there is a reminder of the proof (حجة) of Allah, the Glorified, for inviting them to His worship (عبادة), and an encouragement (ترغيب) for them to respond to the call. As for the reminder of the proof, it points to the fact that they are His servants and He is their Master (المولى); it is the right of the Master over His servant that the servant obeys and worships Him, so He has the right to call them to His obedience and worship. As for encouraging them to respond to the call, it lies in the attribution of them to Himself, the Exalted, which motivates them to cling to the hem of His mercy and forgiveness.

His saying: الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ "who have committed excesses against their own souls." Extravagance (الإسراف) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is exceeding the limit in every act a human performs, though it is most famous in spending. The verb here implies the meaning of a transgression (جناية) or something close to it, which is why it is followed by the preposition "against" (على). Extravagance against the soul is to transgress against it by committing sin (الذنب), whether it be polytheism (الشرك) or other major and minor sins, as the context suggests.

A group of commentators said: What is meant by "servants" (العباد) here are the believers (المؤمنون), as its usage when attributed to Allah, the Exalted, in the Quran is predominantly for them. Thus, the meaning of "O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls" would be: "O sinning believers."

This is refuted by the fact that His saying: "O My servants who have committed excesses..." until the end of the seven verses, possesses a single, connected context (سياق) that articulates their invitation. His saying at the conclusion of the verses: بَلى قَدْ جاءَتْكَ آياتِي فَكَذَّبْتَ بِها وَاسْتَكْبَرْتَ "‘Yes! My signs certainly came to you, but you denied them and were arrogant...’" (39:59) is nearly explicit—or rather, it is explicit—that "servants" includes the polytheists (المشركين).

The instances in His speech, the Exalted, where the term "My servants" (عِبادِيَ) appears and refers specifically to the believers are about a dozen, all of which are surrounded by contextual clues (قرينة). It is not such that it automatically refers to the believers when used absolutely (الإطلاق), just as the instances where it is used absolutely to mean both polytheists and believers in His speech are also contextual.

In short, that "My servants" in this verse includes the polytheists is something that should not be doubted. The view that it refers to the polytheists specifically, in view of the context of the verses—as narrated from Ibn Abbas—is closer to acceptance than restricting it to the believers.

His saying: لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ "do not despair of the mercy of Allah." Despair (القنوط) is hopelessness (اليأس). What is meant by "mercy" (الرحمة), by the context of addressing the sinners and inviting them, is the mercy related to the Hereafter, rather than the more general mercy that encompasses both this world and the Hereafter. It is well known that what the sinners require from the aspects of the mercy of the Hereafter without intermediary is forgiveness (المغفرة). Thus, mercy here means forgiveness; for this reason, the prohibition of despairing of mercy is justified by His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً "Indeed Allah forgives all sins."

In the verse, there is a shift (التفات) from the first person to the third person, where it is said: "Indeed Allah forgives" (إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ) instead of "Indeed I forgive." This is to indicate that He is Allah, to Whom belong the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى), among which is that He is the All-Forgiving, the Most Merciful. It is as if He is saying: "Do not despair of My mercy, for I am Allah; I forgive all sins because Allah is the All-Forgiving, the Most Merciful."

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً "Indeed Allah forgives all sins" is a justification for the prohibition of despair and an announcement that all sins are capable of being forgiven. Thus, forgiveness is general (عامة), but it requires a specific cause (سبب مخصص) and does not occur haphazardly (جزافاً). That which the Quran considers a cause for forgiveness are two things: intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah (1) — and repentance (التوبة). However, what is meant by "Indeed Allah forgives all sins" is not the forgiveness attained through intercession, because intercession does not reach

(1) The discussion regarding it has already passed in the discourses on intercession in the first volume of this book.

polytheism (الشرك) by the explicit text of the Quran in many verses. It has also passed that His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48) refers to intercession, whereas the current verse—namely "Indeed Allah forgives all sins"—concerns polytheism and all other sins.

Therefore, nothing remains except that what is meant is the forgiveness attained through repentance (التوبة). His speech, the Exalted, is explicit regarding the forgiveness of all sins, even polytheism, through repentance.

Furthermore, the seven verses—as you have known—constitute a single discourse with a connected context that prohibits despair—which is a preamble for what follows—and commands repentance, submission (الإسلام), and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح). The first verse is not an independent, disconnected statement from what follows such that one could entertain the possibility that the generality of forgiveness therein is not restricted by repentance or any other assumed cause for forgiveness.

This verse—namely "Indeed Allah forgives all sins"—is a battlefield of opinions among them. A group went toward restricting the generality of forgiveness therein to polytheism and other major sins for which Allah has promised the Fire, while not restricting the generality by repentance; thus, they claim forgiveness only reaches minor sins.

Others went toward the absolute nature (إطلاق) of forgiveness, not restricting it by repentance or any other cause of forgiveness, except that they restricted it from polytheism due to the explicitness of His saying: "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him..." They concluded a generality of forgiveness even if there is no specific cause that gives preference to the forgiven sinner over others, such as repentance or intercession; this is haphazard forgiveness. They have argued for (1) this with several unsound points.

You are well aware that the subject of the verse is polytheism and all other sins. It is known from His speech, the Exalted, that polytheism is not forgiven except through repentance; thus, restricting the absolute nature of forgiveness in the verse to repentance is unavoidable.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنِيبُوا إِلى رَبِّكُمْ وَأَسْلِمُوا لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ "And turn to your Lord penitently and submit to Him before the punishment overtakes you, then you will not be helped" (39:54). This is coordinated (عطف) with His saying: "do not despair." Penitence (الإنابة) to Allah is returning to Him, which is repentance (التوبة). His saying: إِلى رَبِّكُمْ "to your Lord" is an instance of placing a manifest noun in place of a pronoun; the apparent requirement was to say "and turn to Him." The reason for this is to point to the justification, for the criterion in the worship of Allah, the Glorified, is the attribute of Lordship (ربوبية).

What is meant by submission (الإسلام) is surrendering to Allah and yielding to Him in what He wills. He said: وَأَسْلِمُوا لَهُ "and submit to Him" and did not say "and believe in Him" because what is mentioned before and after the verse is their arrogance (استكبار) against the Truth, and the opposite of that is submission.

His saying: مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ "before the punishment overtakes you, then you will not be helped" is connected to "turn penitently" and "submit." What is meant by punishment (العذاب) is the punishment of the Hereafter, by the context of the following verses. It is possible, though remote, that it refers to any punishment with which repentance is no longer accepted, such as the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال). The Exalted said: فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنْفَعُهُمْ إِيمانُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا سُنَّتَ اللهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبادِهِ "But their faith did not benefit them when they sighted Our punishment—[this is] Allah’s precedent which has passed among His servants..." (40:85).

What is meant by His saying: ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ "then you will not be helped" is that forgiveness will not reach you in any way because its cause has not been realized; repentance is assumed to be non-existent, and intercession does not include polytheism.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّبِعُوا أَحْسَنَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ بَغْتَةً وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ "And follow the best of what has been sent down to you from your Lord, before the punishment overtakes you suddenly while you are unaware" (39:55). The address is general to both the believer and the disbeliever, like the preceding addresses, and the Quran has been sent down to both groups together.

In the verse is a command to follow the best of what has been sent down from Allah. It was said: What is meant is following the rulings (الأحكام) of the lawful and the prohibited, excluding the stories. It was said: Following what is commanded and forbidden, such as performing the obligatory and the recommended and avoiding the prohibited and the disliked, excluding the permissible. It was said: Following the firm duties (العزائم), which are the obligations and prohibitions. It was said: Following the abrogating (الناسخ) — the later revelation that replaces a previous ruling — rather than the abrogated (المنسوخ) — the earlier revelation whose ruling has been replaced. It was said: What was sent down is the genus of heavenly books, and the best of them is the Quran; thus, following the best of what was sent down is following the Quran.

In fairness, His saying in the previous verse: "and submit to Him" encompasses the content of each of these views; thus, restricting His saying: "And follow the best of what has been sent down to you" to any one of them is not free from redundant repetition without cause.

Perhaps what is meant by "the best of what has been sent down" are the addresses that point to the way of exercising the right of servitude (حق العبودية) in complying with the divine addresses regarding belief and action. These are like the addresses calling for the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted, with total absorption, and toward His love, and toward godwariness (تقوى) as is His due, and toward the sincerity of religion (إخلاص الدين) for Him. Indeed, following these addresses gives man a "good life" (حياة طيبة), breathes into him the spirit of faith, rectifies his deeds, and enters him into the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, the Exalted—and there is no dignity (كرامة) above that dignity.

His saying: مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ بَغْتَةً وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ "before the punishment overtakes you suddenly while you are unaware" is more suited to this meaning. For the call to an action by frightening one with the suddenness of deprivation and the unexpectedness of an obstacle usually occurs regarding matters where the one called is negligent, comforting himself with "soon" and "perhaps." This meaning is more closely related to the rectification of the inner self (الباطن) than the rectification of the outer self (الظاهر) and the performance of the mere shells of deeds. Close to this is His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life. And know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart..." (8:24).

His saying, the Exalted: أَنْ تَقُولَ نَفْسٌ يا حَسْرَتى عَلى ما فَرَّطْتُ فِي جَنْبِ اللهِ "Lest anyone should say, ‘Alas for my negligence with regard to Allah...’" (39:56). It is said in al-Majma‘: Negligence (التفريط) is the neglect of what must be prioritized until its time passes. He said: Regret (التحسر) is the grief over what has passed its time because of its being cut off from him in a way that cannot be rectified. End quote. Al-Raghib said: The side (الجنب) is the limb. He said: Then it is used metaphorically for the direction adjacent to it, following their custom in using other limbs metaphorically for that, such as the right and the left. End quote. So "the side of Allah" (جنب الله) is His side and His direction; it is that which pertains to Him, the Exalted, regarding what is obligatory upon the servant to deal with. The instantiation (مصداق) of that is to worship Him alone and not to disobey Him. Negligence in the side of Allah is shortcoming in that.

His saying: وَإِنْ كُنْتُ لَمِنَ السَّاخِرِينَ "and indeed I was among those who ridiculed" (39:56). The word "that" (أَنْ) is the lightened form of the heavy particle. The ridiculers (الساخرين) is the active participle of "ridiculed" (سخر), meaning mocked (استهزاء).

The meaning of the verse is: We only address you with this discourse as a warning lest a soul among you should say: "Alas for my shortcoming in the side of Allah, and indeed I was among the mockers." The place of this saying is the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ تَقُولَ لَوْ أَنَّ اللهَ هَدانِي لَكُنْتُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ "Or say, ‘Had Allah guided me, I would surely have been among the God-conscious’" (39:57). The pronoun in "say" refers to the soul. What is meant by guidance (الهداية) is instruction and showing the way. The meaning is clear, and it is for the cutting off of excuses.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ تَقُولَ حِينَ تَرَى الْعَذابَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِي كَرَّةً فَأَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "Or say, when it sights the punishment, ‘If only I could have another turn, I would be among the virtuous’" (39:58). The word "if" (لو) is for wishing (التمني), and the "turn" (الكرة) is the return (الرجعة). The meaning is: Or a soul should say, wishing when it sees the punishment on the Day of Resurrection: "If only I had a return to the world, so I could be among the virtuous (المحسنين)."

His saying, the Exalted: بَلى قَدْ جاءَتْكَ آياتِي فَكَذَّبْتَ بِها وَاسْتَكْبَرْتَ وَكُنْتَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ "‘Yes! My signs certainly came to you, but you denied them and were arrogant and you were among the faithless’" (39:59). This is a rebuttal to it and a response specifically to its second saying: "Had Allah guided me, I would surely have been among the God-conscious." The place of the response is the Day of Resurrection, just as the place of the saying is that Day, and the context of the response bears witness to this.

A separation was made between its saying and the response by His saying: "Or say, when it sights..." etc., and He did not respond except to its saying: "Had Allah guided me..." etc.

The reason for the separation is that the three sayings narrated from it are arranged according to the order of their issuance from the criminals on the Day of Resurrection. When the Resurrection occurs and the criminals see that the Day is the Day of Recompense for deeds, and they had neglected them and their time had passed, they will feel regret for what they neglected and cry out with remorse over their negligence: "Alas for my negligence!" The Exalted said: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَتْهُمُ السَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً قالُوا يا حَسْرَتَنا عَلى ما فَرَّطْنا فِيها "...until when the Hour overtakes them suddenly, they will say, ‘Alas for us, for what we neglected in it!’" (6:31).

Then, when they are reckoned and the God-conscious are ordered to enter Paradise and it is said: وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ "‘Get apart today, O you guilty ones!’" (36:59), they will make excuses with their saying: "Had Allah guided me, I would surely have been among the God-conscious."

Then, when they are ordered to enter the Fire and are made to stand before it, then entered into it, they will wish for a return to the world to do good therein so they might be felicitous: "Or say, when it sights the punishment, ‘If only I could have another turn...’" The Exalted said: وَلَوْ تَرى إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَى النَّارِ فَقالُوا يا لَيْتَنا نُرَدُّ وَلا نُكَذِّبَ بِآياتِ رَبِّنا وَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "Were you to see when they are made to stand by the Fire, and they say, ‘Would that we were sent back, and then we would not deny the signs of our Lord and we would be among the believers!’" (6:27). And He said, narrating from them: رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ "‘Our Lord! Bring us out of it; then if we return [to evil], we shall indeed be wrongdoers’" (23:107).

Thus, when He narrated the sayings according to the order between them, He took up the response. Had He delayed the saying responded to until it was adjacent to the response, or brought the response forward until it was adjacent to it, the order (النظم) would have been disrupted (1). He specifically addressed their second saying: "Had Allah guided me..." etc., with the response and refrained from responding to their first and third sayings because in the first there is mention of their mocking the Truth and its people, and in the third their wishing for a return to the world. Allah, the Glorified, rebukes these people on the Day of Resurrection and prevents them from speaking to Him, and does not respond to their speech, as indicated by His saying: قالُوا رَبَّنا غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنا شِقْوَتُنا وَكُنَّا قَوْماً ضالِّينَ رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ قالَ اخْسَؤُا فِيها وَلا تُكَلِّمُونِ إِنَّهُ كانَ فَرِيقٌ مِنْ عِبادِي يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا آمَنَّا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّاحِمِينَ فَاتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا حَتَّى أَنْسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِي وَكُنْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ تَضْحَكُونَ إِنِّي جَزَيْتُهُمُ الْيَوْمَ بِما صَبَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ "They will say, ‘Our Lord! Our wretchedness overcame us, and we were a straying lot. Our Lord! Bring us out of it; then if we return [to evil], we shall indeed be wrongdoers.’ He will say, ‘Begone into it, and do not speak to Me! Indeed there was a part of My servants who would say, ‘‘Our Lord! We have believed. So forgive us, and have mercy on us, and You are the best of the merciful.’’ But you took them in ridicule until they made you forget My remembrance, and you used to laugh at them. Indeed I have rewarded them today for their patience: they are the triumphant’" (23:106-111).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ تَرَى الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا عَلَى اللهِ وُجُوهُهُمْ مُسْوَدَّةٌ أَلَيْسَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوىً لِلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ "On the Day of Resurrection you will see those who lied about Allah with their faces blackened. Is not the abode of the arrogant in hell?" (39:60). Lying about Allah is the claim that He has a partner or that He has a son, and among it is innovation (البدعة) in religion.

The blackness of the face is a sign of humiliation (الذلة), which is the recompense for their arrogance; for this reason, He said: "Is not the abode of the arrogant in hell?"

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُنَجِّي اللهُ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا بِمَفازَتِهِمْ لا يَمَسُّهُمُ السُّوءُ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ "Allah will deliver those who were God-conscious with their triumph: no harm shall touch them, nor shall they grieve" (39:61). It appears that "triumph" (مفازة) is a mim-infinitive meaning success (الفوز), which is the attainment of what is desired. The preposition "with" (بـ) in "with their triumph" (بِمَفازَتِهِمْ) is for accompaniment or causality; thus, the success that Allah decrees for them today is the cause of their deliverance.

His saying: لا يَمَسُّهُمُ "no harm shall touch them..." etc., is an explanation of their deliverance, as if it were said: He delivers them such that no harm touches them from without, nor do they grieve within themselves.

The verse has a view toward His saying, the Exalted, at the conclusion of the verses of Surah al-Mu'minun quoted just now: "Indeed I have rewarded them today for their patience: they are the triumphant." Reflect and do not be heedless.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, narrated from Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) that he said: "There is no verse in the Quran more encompassing than: يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ (O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls...)."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Jarir from Ibn Sirin from him (peace be upon him). And there will come, Allah willing, in the commentary of Surah al-Layl, the narration from him (peace be upon him) that His saying, the Exalted: وَلَسَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضى "And soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased" (93:5) is more hopeful than this verse.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Iman extracted from Thawban who said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "I would not love to have the world and all that is in it in exchange for this verse: يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ until the end of the verse." A man said: "O Messenger of Allah, what about one who has committed polytheism?" The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained silent, then said: "Except for one who has committed polytheism."

I say: There is something in this narration, for it has already passed that the subject of the verse is polytheism and that the verse is restricted by repentance.

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah and Muslim extracted from Abu Ayyub al-Ansari who said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "Were it not that you commit sins, Allah would have created a creation that commits sins so that He might forgive them."

I say: The forgiveness mentioned in the hadith does not refuse being restricted by the causes of forgiveness such as repentance and intercession.

In the same source, it was said: This verse—meaning His saying: "O My servants who have committed excesses..." etc.—was revealed regarding Wahshi, the killer of Hamzah, when he wanted to submit to Islam but feared that his repentance would not be accepted. When the verse was revealed, he submitted.

It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah, is this for him specifically or for the Muslims generally?" He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) replied: "Rather, for the Muslims generally."

From the book Sa‘d al-Su‘ud by Ibn Tawus, quoting from the Tafsir of al-Kalbi: Wahshi and a group sent word to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "Nothing prevents us from your religion except that we heard you reciting in your Book that whoever calls upon another god with Allah, kills a soul, and commits adultery will meet a penalty and abide forever in punishment—and we have done all of that." So He sent to them His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً "excepting those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously" (25:70). They said: "We fear that we might not act righteously."

So He sent to them: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48). They said: "We fear that we might not be included in the wish." So He sent to them: يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً "O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah; indeed Allah forgives all sins." So they came and submitted.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to Wahshi, the killer of Hamzah: "Hide your face from me, for I cannot bear to look at you." He said: So he went to Syria and died in al-Khamr.

I say: Something similar was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through several chains, and in some of them, it is stated that His saying: "O My servants who have committed excesses..." etc., was revealed regarding him as in the report of al-Majma‘ mentioned earlier. This is weakened by the fact that the Surah is Meccan while Wahshi submitted after the Hijrah. Furthermore, the apparent sense of the report is the lack of restriction of the absolute nature of forgiveness in the verse by repentance, and you have known that the context refuses this.

His saying: "he died in al-Khamr" (فمات في الخمر)—perhaps with a fathah on the kha and a shaddah on the mim—is a place in the outskirts of Medina. Perhaps it is a mistake of the people, and the correct name is Homs. Or perhaps what is meant is his death due to drinking wine (الخمر), for he was addicted to wine and was lashed for it more than once, then he stopped.

Know that there are many narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) regarding the application (تطبيق) of these verses to their Shia and the application of "the side of Allah" (جنب الله) to them; all of these are of the nature of "flow" (الجري) — the application of a verse to new instances that fit its general meaning — rather than formal exegesis (التفسير). For this reason, we have omitted their inclusion here.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ (٦٢)
Allah is creator of all things, and He watches over all things. (62)
لَّهُۥ مَقَالِيدُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٦٣)
To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth, and those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah—it is they who are the losers. (63)
قُلْ أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَأْمُرُوٓنِّىٓ أَعْبُدُ أَيُّهَا ٱلْجَـٰهِلُونَ (٦٤)
Say, ‘Will you, then, bid me to worship other than Allah, O you ignorant ones?!’ (64)
وَلَقَدْ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكَ لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٦٥)
Certainly it has been revealed to you and to those [who have been] before you: ‘If you ascribe a partner to Allah your works shall fail and you shall surely be among the losers. (65)
بَلِ ٱللَّهَ فَٱعْبُدْ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (٦٦)
No, worship Allah, and be among the grateful!’ (66)
وَمَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْضُ جَمِيعًا قَبْضَتُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ مَطْوِيَّـٰتٌۢ بِيَمِينِهِۦ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٧)
They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him, yet the entire earth will be in His fist on the Day of Resurrection, and the heavens, scrolled, in His right hand. Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (67)
وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرَىٰ فَإِذَا هُمْ قِيَامٌ يَنظُرُونَ (٦٨)
And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens will swoon and whoever is on the earth, except whomever Allah wishes. Then it will be blown a second time, behold, they will rise up, looking on! (68)
وَأَشْرَقَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّهَا وَوُضِعَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ وَجِا۟ىٓءَ بِٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءِ وَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٦٩)
The earth will glow with the light of her Lord, and the Book will be set up, and the prophets and the martyrs will be brought, and judgment will be made between them with justice, and they will not be wronged. (69)
وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٧٠)
Every soul will be recompensed fully for what it has done, and He is best aware of what they do. (70)
وَسِيقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ زُمَرًا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُوهَا فُتِحَتْ أَبْوَٰبُهَا وَقَالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُهَآ أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ يَتْلُونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكُمْ وَيُنذِرُونَكُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا ۚ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنْ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ ٱلْعَذَابِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧١)
The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs. When they reach it and its gates are opened, its keepers will say to them, ‘Did there not come to you [any] apostles from among yourselves, reciting to you the signs of your Lord and warning you of the encounter of this day of yours?’ They will say, ‘Yes, but the word of punishment became due against the faithless.’ (71)
قِيلَ ٱدْخُلُوٓا۟ أَبْوَٰبَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٧٢)
It will be said, ‘Enter the gates of hell to remain in it [forever]. Evil is the [ultimate] abode of the arrogant.’ (72)
وَسِيقَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْجَنَّةِ زُمَرًا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُوهَا وَفُتِحَتْ أَبْوَٰبُهَا وَقَالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُهَا سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ طِبْتُمْ فَٱدْخُلُوهَا خَـٰلِدِينَ (٧٣)
Those who are wary of their Lord will be led to paradise in throngs. When they reach it and its gates are opened, its keepers will say to them, ‘Peace be to you! You are welcome! Enter it to remain [forever].’ (73)
وَقَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى صَدَقَنَا وَعْدَهُۥ وَأَوْرَثَنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشَآءُ ۖ فَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ (٧٤)
They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us inheritors of the earth, that we may settle in paradise wherever we may wish!’ How excellent is the reward of the workers [of righteousness]! (74)
وَتَرَى ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ حَآفِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ ٱلْعَرْشِ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ ۖ وَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَقِيلَ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧٥)
And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne, celebrating the praise of their Lord, and judgment will be made between them with justice, and it will be said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!’ (75)

(Commentary)

This is a section of verses with which the Surah concludes. It mentions a summary of what is yielded by the arguments mentioned therein previously. Then, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is commanded to address the polytheists (المشركين), stating that their proposal to him to worship their gods is nothing but ignorance of His (the Exalted) station. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) mentions what was revealed to him and to those before him: "If you ascribe partners [to Allah], your work will surely fail."

Then, He (the Glorified) mentions that the polytheists did not recognize Him with the recognition due to Him; otherwise, they would not have doubted His Lordship (الربوبية) over them, nor would they have worshipped other than Him. Then, He (the Exalted) mentions the system of returning to Him, which is the governance (تدبير) of the aspect of the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final destination of creation — from the perspective of creation, with a comprehensive and sufficient explanation beyond which nothing more is needed. He concludes the Surah with praise (الحمد).

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Allah is the creator of all things" (39:62). This is what He mentioned regarding the polytheists' acknowledgment of Him previously in His saying: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ — "If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’" (39:38). He built upon this the dependence of all things upon Him in their governance (تدبير).

The sentence in this context serves as a preamble to what is mentioned after it regarding the fact that governance is dependent upon Him, because—as has been mentioned repeatedly—creation (الخلق) is inseparable from governance (التدبير). Thus, the discourse transitions here from the dependence of creation upon Him to the exclusivity of sovereignty (الملك) to Him, which is His saying: لَهُ مَقالِيدُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth" (39:63). And from the exclusivity of sovereignty to Him, it transitions to His being the Trustee (الوكيل) over everything, the One who stands in place of all things in managing their affairs.

A discussion regarding the meaning of the universality of creation (خلقة) for everything has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "That is Allah, your Lord, there is no god except Him, the creator of all things" (6:102) in the seventh volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ — "and He is trustee over all things" (39:62). This is because the fact that the creation and existence of every thing terminates in Him necessitates that He (the Exalted) be the Owner (المالك) of everything. Thus, no thing possesses anything—neither itself nor anything that emanates from itself—except through Allah (the Exalted) granting it ownership. Therefore, due to its absolute poverty (فقر), it does not possess its own governance, while Allah is the Owner of its governance.

As for His (the Exalted) granting it ownership of itself and its actions, this is also a type of His governance that affirms His ownership, neither negating nor contradicting the aspects of His trusteeship (وكالة) over them. It is not a delegation (تفويض) of the matter [to the creature] nor an invalidation of the trusteeship; so understand this.

In short, since no thing possesses anything for itself, He (the Glorified) is the Trustee (الوكيل) over it, the One who stands in its place and governs its affairs. In this regard, causes (الأسباب) and effects (المسببات) are equal; Allah (the Glorified) is their Lord alone.

Thus, it has become clear that the sentence is intended to point toward His uniqueness in Lordship (الربوبية), which is the intended point of explanation. Therefore, the statement of some—that mentioning this after His saying: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ is to indicate that He is the Absolutely Self-Sufficient (الغني المطلق) and that benefits and harms return to the servants, or that the meaning is that He (the Exalted) is the Preserver (حفيظ) over everything, thereby indicating that things are in need of Him for their subsistence (بقاء) just as they are in need of Him for their origination (حدوث)—is entirely foreign to the meaning of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُ مَقالِيدُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth" (39:63). The "keys" (المقاليد), as has been said, means the openers (المفاتيح), and it has no singular form from its own root.

The keys of the heavens and the earth are the keys to their treasuries (خزائن). He (the Exalted) said: وَلِلَّهِ خَزائِنُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "and to Allah belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth" (63:7). Their treasuries are their Unseen (غيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception — from which things and the system governing them appear, thus emerging into the manifest world (الشهادة). He (the Exalted) said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ — "There is not a thing but that its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21).

Possessing the keys (مقاليد) of the heavens and the earth is a metaphor for possessing their treasuries, from which come the existences of things, their provisions, their lifespans, their appointed times, and everything else they encounter in their journey from the moment they begin from Him (the Exalted) until the moment they return to Him.

This phrase—meaning His saying: لَهُ مَقالِيدُ etc.—is in the position of providing the reason (تعليل) for His saying: وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ. For this reason, it was brought as a separate sentence without a conjunction.

And His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ — "As for those who defy the signs of Allah, it is they who are the losers" (39:63). It has already been mentioned that His saying: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ up to وَالْأَرْضِ mentions the summary of what the arguments mentioned throughout the previous verses provide. Accordingly, His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ etc., is conjoined to His saying: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ. The meaning indicated by the preceding verses and arguments is that Allah (the Glorified) is the Creator, then the Owner, then the Trustee over everything—meaning He is unique in Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية) — the state of being the sole object of worship — and those who disbelieved in the signs of their Lord, thus failing to unify Him or worship Him, it is they who are the losers (الخاسرون).

Scholars have differed regarding what His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا etc., is conjoined to, mentioning many different views that are of no benefit. Whoever desires them should refer to the lengthy commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَفَغَيْرَ اللهِ تَأْمُرُونِّي أَعْبُدُ أَيُّهَا الْجاهِلُونَ — "Say, ‘Do you bid me worship other than Allah, you ignorant ones?’" (39:64). When He (the Glorified) presented the summary of what the arguments mentioned in the Surah articulate regarding His uniqueness in creation, ownership, and governance—and the necessary consequence of that is His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity—He commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to address the polytheists who proposed that he worship their gods. He was to tell them that with these brilliant and manifest arguments, there remains no place for worshipping other than Allah or responding to their proposal; and is it anything but ignorance (الجهل)?

Thus, His saying: أَفَغَيْرَ اللهِ تَأْمُرُونِّي أَعْبُدُ, the letter fa (الفاء) is for branching the content of the sentence from His saying: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ to the end of the two verses. The interrogation is for denial (إنكاري). The phrase فَغَيْرَ اللهِ is the object (مفعول) of أَعْبُدُ, placed before it for emphasis. The word تَأْمُرُونِّي is parenthetical between the verb and its object; its origin is ta’murunani, where one of the two nuns was elided into the other.

His saying: أَيُّهَا الْجاهِلُونَ — "O you ignorant ones" — addresses them with the attribute of ignorance to indicate that their commanding him to worship other than Allah and their proposal of that, despite the manifestation of the signs of His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity, is nothing but ignorance on their part.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ etc. — "It has already been revealed to you and to those before you: ‘If you ascribe partners [to Allah], your work will surely fail...’" (39:65). In this is a reinforcement of the content of the mentioned rational arguments through revelation (الوحي). It is as if it were said: Do not worship other than Allah, for it is ignorance; and how could it be permissible for you to worship [another] when revelation has indicated its prohibition just as reason has indicated it?

Thus, His saying: وَلَقَدْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ, the letter lam (اللام) is for an oath (القسم). His saying: لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ is an explanation of what was revealed to him. The implied meaning of the speech is: "I swear, it has been revealed to you that if you ascribe partners... etc., and to those before you of the prophets and messengers: if you [all] ascribe partners, your work will surely fail and you will surely be among the losers."

Addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the rest of the prophets (عليه السلام) with the prohibition of polytheism (الشرك) and warning them of the nullification of deeds (حبط العمل) and entering the ranks of the losers is an address and a warning in their literal sense. How could it be otherwise? The purpose of the Surah—as previously indicated—is to explain that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is commanded to believe in what he calls the polytheists to believe in, and is obligated with what he obligates them with; he cannot possibly grant their proposal to worship their gods.

As for the prophets being protected by divine inerrancy (العصمة) — the divinely granted immunity from sin and error — which makes the commission of a sin impossible for them, this does not necessitate the falling away of obligation (التكليف) from them or the invalidity of directing it toward them. If that were the case, sin would not be conceivable in their regard, like all those who have no obligation; thus, there would be no meaning to their inerrancy.

Furthermore, inerrancy—which is a power that makes the commission of sin impossible—is among the affairs of the station of knowledge (العلم), as previously indicated in the commentary on His saying: وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يُضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they cannot do you any harm" (4:113). It does not contradict the established free will (الاختيار), which is among the affairs of the station of action (العمل), and the possibility of the occurrence of performance or omission by the limbs.

Thus, the prevention by certain knowledge (العلم القطعي) of the corruption of a thing—preventing its occurrence from the one who knows it with absolute certainty—is like the prevention by knowledge of the effect of poison from drinking it. This does not contradict the fact that the one who knows it is acting by choice (مختار) in the action, due to the possibility of its occurrence and its issuance from his limbs. Therefore, inerrancy does not contradict obligation in any way.

From what has preceded, the weakness of what is understood from some—that the prohibition of him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from polytheism and the like is a formal prohibition, while the intended target is his community (أمة), being of the nature of "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor"—becomes apparent.

The reason for this weakness is clear from what has preceded. As for our saying—as mentioned in some narrations—that these Quranic addresses are of the nature of "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor," its meaning is that since the outward nature of obligation is to be attached to one for whom obedience and disobedience are possible, if it is attached to one from whom nothing but obedience proceeds while others share the situation with him, that would be an obligation in a more eloquent manner, like a metaphor (الكناية) which is more eloquent than explicit statement.

And His saying: وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ — "and you will surely be among the losers" (39:65). Its meaning has become clear from what preceded. It is possible that the lam in "the losers" (الخاسرين) denotes a specific reference (العهد), meaning: "and you will surely be among those losers who disbelieved in the signs of Allah and turned away from the arguments indicating His oneness."

His saying, the Exalted: بَلِ اللهَ فَاعْبُدْ وَكُنْ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ — "Rather worship Allah and be among the grateful" (39:66). This is a transition (إضراب) from the prohibition understood from the preceding speech, as if it were said: "Do not worship other than Allah; rather, worship Allah." The placement of the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة) first is to indicate restriction (الحصر).

The fa (الفاء) in فَاعْبُدْ is extra for emphasis, according to what has been said. It has also been said that it is the fa of recompense (الجزاء) whose condition (شرط) has been omitted, the implied meaning being: "Rather, if you are a worshipper or a rational being, then worship Allah."

His saying: وَكُنْ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ — "and be among the grateful" — means: be, through your worship of Him, among those who thank Him for His blessings that indicate His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity. It has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: وَسَيَجْزِي اللهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ — "and Allah will soon reward the grateful" (3:144) and His saying: وَلا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شاكِرِينَ — "and You will not find most of them to be grateful" (7:17), that the referent of "the grateful" (الشاكرين) in the true sense of the word are the sincere ones (المخلصون) — those purified by Allah for Himself — so refer back to that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ — "They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him" (39:67) to the end of the verse. The "measure" (قدر) of a thing is its amount and quantity in terms of size, number, weight, or the like. Then it was used metaphorically for spiritual matters such as status and rank.

Thus, His saying: وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ is a representation (تمثيل) intended to mean their lack of recognizing Him (the Exalted) with the recognition due to Him, as they did not recognize Him from the perspective of the Return (المعاد) and the return of all things to Him. This is indicated by following the sentence with His saying: وَالْأَرْضُ جَمِيعاً قَبْضَتُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ — "the entire earth will be in His fist on the Day of Resurrection" — to the end of the Surah, where He mentions the severing of every cause besides Him on the Day of Resurrection. He mentions His grasping of the earth, His folding of the heavens, the blowing of the Trumpet to cause the death of all and then to revive them, the earth shining with the light of its Lord, the laying down of the Book, the bringing of the prophets and the witnesses, the judgment, the paying in full to every soul what it has done, and the driving of the criminals to the Fire and the God-conscious to Paradise. Whoever's state in sovereignty and disposal is this state, and is recognized by that, this recognition necessitates turning toward Him by worshipping Him alone and turning away from all others entirely.

However, since the polytheists did not believe in the Return, they did not regard Him with the regard due to Him and did not recognize Him with the recognition due to Him; thus, they turned away from His worship to the worship of others.

His saying: وَالْأَرْضُ جَمِيعاً قَبْضَتُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ — "the entire earth will be in His fist on the Day of Resurrection" — means the earth with all its parts and the causes acting upon one another within it. The "fist" (القبضة) is a verbal noun meaning "that which is grasped." Grasping a thing and its being in the fist is a metaphor for complete mastery over it, or the restriction of mastery over it to the one grasping. What is intended here is the second meaning, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ — "and on that day all command will belong to Allah" (82:19) and other verses.

It has been mentioned repeatedly that the meaning of the restriction of sovereignty, command, judgment, authority, and the like to Him (the Exalted) on the Day of Resurrection is the manifestation of that to the people of the gathering on that day; otherwise, they belong to Him (the Exalted) always. Thus, the meaning of the entire earth being in His fist on the Day of Resurrection is the manifestation of that on that day to the people, not its origin.

His saying: وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ — "and the heavens will be folded up in His right hand" (39:67). The "right hand" (يمين) of a thing is its right hand and its strong side, and it is used as a metaphor for power (القدرة). It is understood from the context that the sum of the two sentences—meaning His saying: وَالْأَرْضُ جَمِيعاً قَبْضَتُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ—is the severing of earthly and heavenly causes, their falling away, and the manifestation that there is no effective agent in existence except Allah (the Glorified).

His saying: سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ — "Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners they ascribe [to Him]!" (39:67). This is a sanctification of Him (the Exalted) from their ascribing others as partners in His Lordship and Divinity, whereby they attributed the governance of the world to their gods and worshipped them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ etc. — "The Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon, except those whom Allah wishes..." (39:68). The apparent meaning of what has been mentioned in His (the Exalted) speech regarding the blowing of the Trumpet is that there are two blowings: a blowing for causing death and a blowing for revival. This is what is indicated by the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) and some of what has been narrated through Sunni channels from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), although some other narrations of theirs are not free from ambiguity. For this reason, some have chosen the view that there are three blowings: a blowing for death, a blowing for revival and resurrection, and a blowing for terror and swooning. Others have said there are four blowings. However, proving that from the outward meanings of the verses is as difficult as "stripping the thorns from a tragacanth tree" (خرط القتاد).

Perhaps the restriction of the blowing to the two blowings of death and revival is what necessitated their interpreting "swooning" (الصعق) in the first blowing as death, even though the well-known meaning of sa‘q is fainting (الغشية). It is said in al-Sihah: It is said: صعق the man sa‘qan and tasā‘uqan, meaning he fainted, and another made him faint (as‘aqahu). Then he said: And His saying, the Exalted: فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ means they died. End quote.

His saying: إِلاَّ مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ — "except those whom Allah wishes" — is an exception from the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth. There is disagreement as to who they are.

It has been said: They are Jibra’il, Mika’il, Israfil, and ‘Azra’il, the masters of the angels, for they only die after that. It has been said: They are these four and the Bearers of the Throne (حملة العرش). It has been said: They are Ridwan, the huris, Malik, and the angels of punishment (الزبانية). It has been said—and this is the weakest of opinions—that the one intended by "whom Allah wishes" is Allah (the Glorified). You are well aware that none of these statements is based on evidence from the wording of the verses that can be relied upon.

Yes, if one were to conceive of a creation for Allah (the Glorified) beyond the heavens and the earth, it would be permissible to exclude them from their inhabitants as a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع). Or it could be said: Death only overtakes bodies by the severing of the souls' attachment to them, whereas the souls do not die; thus, the souls are the ones excluded as a connected exception (استثناء متصل). This view is supported by some (1) narrations related from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

(1) Such as what was narrated regarding His saying, the Exalted: "To whom belongs the sovereignty today?" (40:16), that the answer: "To Allah, the One, the All-paramount" is from the souls of the prophets, and other such narrations.

His saying: ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرى فَإِذا هُمْ قِيامٌ يَنْظُرُونَ — "Then it will be blown another time, and behold, they will be standing, looking on!" (39:68). The pronoun in فِيهِ refers to the Trumpet (الصور). The word أُخْرى is an adjective for an omitted noun, meaning "another blowing" (نفخة أخرى). The word قيام is the plural of "standing" (قائم). The word يَنْظُرُونَ means they are waiting (ينتظرون) or it is from "looking" (النظر) in its well-known sense.

The meaning is: The Trumpet will be blown another time, and suddenly they will be standing from their graves, waiting for what they are commanded, or waiting to see what will be done with them, or suddenly they will be standing, looking with the gaze of one who is stunned and bewildered.

What is in this verse—that after the blowing they will be standing and looking—does not contradict what is in His saying: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَإِذا هُمْ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يَنْسِلُونَ — "And when the Trumpet is blown, behold, they will scramble out of the graves toward their Lord!" (36:51), meaning they hasten; and His saying: يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَتَأْتُونَ أَفْواجاً — "the day the Trumpet is blown, and you come in groups" (78:18); and His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَفَزِعَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "On the day the Trumpet is blown, whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will be terrified" (27:87). For their terror at the blowing, their hastening in walking to the arena of the Gathering, and their coming to it in groups are like their standing and looking—events that are close in time and do not negate one another.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَشْرَقَتِ الْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّها — "The earth will shine with the light of its Lord" (39:69) to the end of the verse. The "shining" (إشراق) of the earth is its illumination. "Light" (النور) is well-known in meaning. Light has been used in His (the Exalted) speech for physical light many times, and it has also been applied to faith (الإيمان) and to the Quran, with the consideration that each of them manifests to the one characterized by it what would have been hidden from him without it. He (the Exalted) said: اللهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ — "Allah is the wali of the faithful: He brings them out of darkness into light" (2:257), and He said: فَآمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَالنُّورِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلْنا — "So have faith in Allah and His Apostle and the light which We have sent down" (64:8).

Scholars have differed regarding the meaning of the earth shining with the light of its Lord. It has been said: It shines with a light that Allah creates without the mediation of luminous bodies like the sun and the moon, and its attribution to Him (the Exalted) is like "My spirit" and "the she-camel of Allah."

The problem with this is that it is not based on any evidence that can be relied upon.

It has been said: What is intended is the manifestation (تجلي) of the Lord (the Exalted) for the passing of judgment, as mentioned in some reports from Sunni channels.

The problem with this is that, assuming the narration is authentic, it does not prove the claim.

It has been said: What is intended is the illumination of the earth with the justice (عدل) of its Lord on the Day of Resurrection, because the light of the earth is through justice, just as the light of knowledge is through action.

The problem with this is that the correctness of using light as a metaphor for justice in itself does not necessitate that light in the verse means justice, except with evidence indicating it, and none has been provided.

In al-Kashshaf: Allah (Mighty and Majestic) has used light as a metaphor for truth (الحق) and proof (البرهان) in several places in the Revelation, and this is one of them. The meaning is: the earth will shine with the truth and justice that He establishes within it, and the equity (القسط) that He spreads in the reckoning and the weighing of good and evil deeds.

It is signaled as being metaphorical by its attribution to His name, because He is the Truth, the Just. And the attribution of His name to the earth is because He beautifies it when He spreads His justice within it, sets up His scales of equity, and judges with truth among its people. You do not see anything more beautifying for lands than justice, nor anything more restorative for them than it. In this attribution is the point that its Lord and Creator is the One who acts justly within it, while only other than its Lord acts unjustly within it. Furthermore, what is conjoined to the shining of the earth—the laying down of the Book, the bringing of the prophets and the witnesses, and the judging with truth—is the aforementioned light. You see people saying to a just king: "The horizons have shone with your justice, and the world has been illuminated by your equity," just as you say: "The lands have become dark with the injustice of so-and-so." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Injustice (الظلم) is darkness (ظلمات) on the Day of Resurrection." And just as He opened the verse by affirming justice, He closed it by negating injustice. End quote.

The problem with this is, firstly: His statement that light is used as a metaphor in many places in the Quran for truth, the Quran, and proof—its use as a metaphor for truth and proof is not apparent in any of the verses.

Secondly: Truth (الحق) and justice (العدل) are two distinct concepts, even if they sometimes coincide. The fact that light in the verse is a metaphor for truth does not necessitate that justice is intended by it. Therefore, when he wanted to explain the intention of justice from light, he mentioned truth along with justice, then concluded for justice rather than truth.

It is not unlikely that what is intended—and Allah knows best—by the earth shining with the light of its Lord is what is characteristic of the Day of Resurrection: the lifting of the veil (انكشاف الغطاء), the appearance of things in their realities, and the manifestation of deeds—whether good or evil, obedience or disobedience, truth or falsehood—to the onlookers. The "shining" (إشراق) of a thing is its appearance through light. There is no doubt that their Manifester on that day is Allah (the Glorified), as causes have fallen away besides Him; thus, things are shining with a light acquired from Him (the Exalted).

This shining, although general for everything that light can encompass, since the purpose was to explain the state of the earth and its people on that day, He specified it in the explanation, saying: وَأَشْرَقَتِ الْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّها.

His mentioning it with the title of the Lordship (ربوبية) of the earth is a rebuke to the polytheists who denied His (the Exalted) Lordship over the earth and what is in it.

The "earth" (الأرض) here refers to the earth and what is in it and what pertains to it, just as it was mentioned that the "earth" in His saying: وَالْأَرْضُ جَمِيعاً قَبْضَتُهُ means that.

What we have presented is supported by many places in His (the Exalted) speech, such as His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have suspended your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today" (50:22); and His saying: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ — "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done..." (3:30); and His saying: يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبارَها بِأَنَّ رَبَّكَ أَوْحى لَها يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ النَّاسُ أَشتاتاً لِيُرَوْا أَعْمالَهُمْ فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ — "On that day she will relate her chronicles, for your Lord will have inspired her. On that day mankind will issue forth in various groups to be shown their deeds. So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it" (99:4-8); and many other verses indicating the appearance of deeds, their embodiment (تجسم), the testimony of the limbs, and so on.

His saying: وَوُضِعَ الْكِتابُ — "the Book will be set up" (39:69). It has been said: What is intended is the reckoning (الحساب), which is as you see [unlikely]. It has been said: What is intended is the records of deeds (صحائف الأعمال) upon which the reckoning is based and judgment is passed. It has been said: What is intended is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), and this is supported by His saying, the Exalted: هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "This is Our record; it speaks against you with the truth. Indeed We used to record what you used to do" (45:29).

His saying: وَجِيءَ بِالنَّبِيِّينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ — "and the prophets and the witnesses will be brought" (39:69). As for the prophets, they are brought to be questioned about the delivery of their messages, as suggested by the context. He (the Exalted) said: فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — "We will surely question those to whom [the apostles] were sent, and We will surely question the apostles" (7:6). As for the witnesses (الشهداء), they are the witnesses of deeds, brought to give the testimony they bore. He (the Exalted) said: فَكَيْفَ إِذا جِئْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَجِئْنا بِكَ عَلى هؤُلاءِ شَهِيداً — "So how shall it be, when We bring a witness from every nation and We bring you as a witness to them?" (4:41).

His saying: وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — "and judgment will be made between them with justice, and they will not be wronged" (39:69). The two plural pronouns refer to the people, as known from the context. The judgment between them is the judgment regarding that which they differed in, which is mentioned repeatedly in His (the Exalted) speech. He said: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — "Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ" (10:93).

His saying, the Exalted: وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَفْعَلُونَ — "Every soul will be paid in full for what it has done, and He knows best what they do" (39:70). "Paying in full" (التوفية) is giving completely. It has been attached to the deed itself rather than its reward, which fundamentally cuts off any doubt about it being equity and justice. The verse is in the position of an explanation for His saying: وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ.

His saying: وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَفْعَلُونَ — "and He knows best what they do" — means that His judging in this manner—by setting up the Book and bringing the prophets and witnesses—is not out of ignorance on His part or need, but rather so that His judgment may proceed upon equity and justice; for He knows best what they do.

The previous verse contains the judgment and the ruling, and this verse contains its execution, while the subsequent verses detail its execution.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ — "The faithless will be driven to hell" (39:71) to the end of the verse. "Driving" (السوق) — as in al-Majma‘ — is urging to travel. "Groups" (الزمر) is the plural of zumrah, which is — as in al-Sihah — a group of people.

The meaning is: وَسِيقَ — and they were urged to travel — الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ زُمَراً — those who disbelieved to Hell, group after group — حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها — until they reached it — فُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها — its gates were opened for their entry, and they are seven. He (the Exalted) said: لَها سَبْعَةُ أَبْوابٍ — "It has seven gates" (15:44). وَقالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها — "and its keepers will say to them" — and they are the angels entrusted with it, saying to them by way of shaming and rebuking: أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ — "Did there not come to you apostles from among yourselves" — of your own kind, human beings — يَتْلُونَ — "reciting" — عَلَيْكُمْ آياتِ رَبِّكُمْ — "to you the signs of your Lord" — of the arguments indicating His oneness and the obligation of His worship. قالُوا — "They will say" — "Yes, they came and recited," وَلكِنْ — "but" — we disbelieved and denied, and حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ الْعَذابِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ — "the word of punishment has become due against the faithless." The "word of punishment" is His (the Exalted) saying when He commanded Adam to descend: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ — "As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (2:39).

His saying, the Exalted: قِيلَ ادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ — "It will be said, ‘Enter the gates of hell to remain in it [forever]. Evil is the abode of the arrogant!’" (39:72). The speaker—as indicated by the context—is the keepers of Hell. In His saying: فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ is an indication that these who disbelieved are the deniers of the signs of Allah, the stubborn opponents of the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ زُمَراً حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها وَفُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها — "The God-conscious will be driven to paradise in groups until, when they reach it and its gates are opened..." (39:73). The answer to "when" (إذا) is not mentioned in the verse, indicating that it is a matter beyond description and beyond what can be measured. His saying: وَفُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها is a circumstantial clause (حال), meaning they reached it while its gates had already been opened. His saying: خَزَنَتُها refers to the angels entrusted with it.

The meaning is: وَسِيقَ — and they were urged to travel — الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ زُمَراً — those who were God-conscious toward their Lord to Paradise, group after group — حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها وَ — and [when] — فُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها وَقالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها — its gates were opened and its keepers said to them, welcoming them: سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ — "Peace be upon you!" — you are in absolute peace, nothing shall meet you except what pleases you. طِبْتُمْ — "You have been good" — and perhaps this is the reason for the absolute peace. فَادْخُلُوها خالِدِينَ — "so enter it to remain [forever]" — in it. This is the effect of their goodness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي صَدَقَنا وَعْدَهُ وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ — "They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us heirs to the earth...’" (39:74) to the end of the verse. The speakers are the God-conscious. What is intended by the "promise" (الوعد) is what has been repeated in His (the Exalted) speech and in what was revealed to the rest of the prophets regarding the promise of Paradise to the God-conscious. He said: لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتٌ — "For those who are God-conscious, there are gardens with their Lord" (3:15), and He said: إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ — "Indeed for the God-conscious there are gardens of bliss with their Lord" (68:34). Thus it has been said. It has also been said: What is intended by the promise is the promise of resurrection and reward.

It is not unlikely that what is intended by the promise is the promise of inheriting Paradise, as in His saying: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْوارِثُونَ الَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ الْفِرْدَوْسَ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ — "It is they who will be the inheritors, who will inherit Paradise, and they will remain in it [forever]" (23:10-11). Thus, His saying: وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ would be an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) for His saying: صَدَقَنا وَعْدَهُ.

His saying: وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ — "and made us heirs to the earth." What is intended by the "earth" (الأرض)—according to what they have said—is the earth of Paradise, which is that upon which they settle. It has already preceded at the beginning of Surah al-Mu’minun that the meaning of their inheriting Paradise is its remaining for them after it was in a position to be shared by others or owned by others besides them; but those others passed away from it, so it was transferred to them.

His saying: نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشاءُ — "we settle in paradise wherever we wish" — is an explanation of their inheriting the earth. The replacement of the pronoun for "earth" with "Paradise" is to indicate that it is what is intended by the earth.

It has been said: What is intended by the earth is the earth of the world, but this is feeble unless it is explained that Paradise is the ultimate end of this abode (عقبى هذه الدار). He (the Exalted) said: أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ — "For such there will be the ultimate abode" (13:22).

The meaning is: The God-conscious said after entering Paradise: "All praise belongs to Allah who fulfilled His promise to us that He would enter us into or make us heirs to Paradise; we reside in it wherever we wish and choose"—for they have whatever they wish therein.

His saying: فَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ الْعامِلِينَ — "How excellent is the reward of those who act!" (39:74). That is, how excellent is the reward, the reward of those who act for Allah (the Exalted). According to what the context provides, this is the speech of the people of Paradise. It has also been suggested that it is from the speech of Allah (the Exalted).

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ — "And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne, celebrating the praise of their Lord" (39:75) to the end of the verse. "Surrounding" (الحف) is encircling and encompassing a thing. The "Throne" (العرش) is the station from which the divine decrees and commands are issued, by which the world is governed. The angels are the executors of His will, acting by His command. Seeing the angels in that state is a metaphor for the manifestation of that, after the heavens have been folded.

The meaning is: You will see on that day the angels while they are encircling the Throne, going around it to execute the command issued from it, while they celebrate the praise of their Lord.

His saying: وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ — "and judgment will be made between them" (39:75). It is possible that the pronoun refers to the angels; or it refers to the people and the angels together; or it refers to all creatures; or it refers to the people, so the judgment is between the people of Paradise and the people of Hell among them, or between the prophets and their communities.

The last possibility is weakened by the fact that the judgment between the people has already been mentioned in His saying: وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ. Thus, mentioning the judgment between them a second time would be a repetition without a cause.

However, the apparent meaning of judgment between a group is the ruling for some against others due to the existence of some difference between them, and no difference is realized among the angels. This supports the pronoun being for others, and the judgment being between the people. However, just as "judgment" (القضاء) is applied to the ruling of the judge itself, it is also correct to apply it to the sum of the ruling, its preliminaries, and its consequences—from the presence of the litigants, the presentation of the case, the testimony of the witnesses, the ruling of the judge, and the giving of the rightful person his due. Thus, it is possible that what is intended by the judgment mentioned first is the divine ruling itself, and by this judgment mentioned second is the sum of what occurs to them from the time they are resurrected until the time the people of the Fire enter the Fire and the people of Paradise enter Paradise and settle therein. By this, the problem of repetition without a cause is removed.

His saying: وَقِيلَ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "and it will be said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds’" (39:75). This is a concluding word for the beginning and the return, and a general praise for Him (the Exalted) that He did not do and does not do anything except what is beautiful.

It has been said: The speakers are the God-conscious; their first praise was for their entry into Paradise, and the second was for the judgment between them and others with truth. It has been said: The speakers are the angels, and it was not explicitly attributed to them to magnify their affair. It has been said: The speakers are all creatures.

The first view is supported by His saying, the Exalted, regarding the description of the people of Paradise: وَآخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "and their concluding call will be, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds’" (10:10). This is a general praise concluding the creation, as you have heard.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ: "This is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), but the meaning is for his community. This is what al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) said: 'Indeed, Allah (Mighty and Majestic) sent His Prophet with [the principle of] "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor."'"

In the book al-Tawhid, with its chain of narration to al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) saying: 'Indeed, Allah (Mighty and Majestic) cannot be described.'"

He said: And Zurarah said: Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) said: "Indeed, Allah cannot be described. And how can He be described when He said in His Book: وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ? Thus, He is not described by any measure except that He is greater than that."

In the same book, with its chain of narration from Sulayman ibn Mehran, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah (Mighty and Majestic): وَالْأَرْضُ جَمِيعاً قَبْضَتُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ. He said: 'It is His sovereignty; no one possesses it with Him.'

And 'grasping' (القبض) from Allah (the Exalted) in another place is withholding (المنع), and 'extending' (البسط) from Him is giving and expansion, as He (Mighty and Majestic) said: وَاللهُ يَقْبِضُ وَيَبْصُطُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ — meaning He gives and expands, and He restricts. And 'grasping' from Him (Mighty and Majestic) in another aspect is taking (الأخذ), and 'taking' in one aspect is acceptance from Him, as He said: وَيأْخُذُ الصَّدَقاتِ — meaning He accepts them from their people and rewards for them.

I said: 'Then what of His saying (Mighty and Majestic): وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ?' He said: 'The right hand (اليمين) is the hand (اليد), and the hand is power (القدرة) and strength (القوة). He (Mighty and Majestic) says: وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ — meaning by His power and His strength, immaculate is He and exalted above what they associate [with Him].'"

I say: It is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu Hurayrah, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ: "That they are the martyrs (الشهداء), girded with their swords around His Throne..." the report. Its outward meaning is that the blowing is other than the blowing of death, but it has already preceded that the verse is apparent in the opposite of that.

It is narrated from Anas, from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "That they are Jibra’il, Mika’il, Israfil, and the Angel of Death—and the Bearers of the Throne—and that they die after it..." the report. The verse is apparent in the opposite of that.

It is narrated from Jabir: "Musa was excluded because he had swooned before..." the report.

The problem with this is that "swooning" (الصعق), whether taken to mean death or fainting, is not restricted to Musa (عليه السلام) before that.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَها سَبْعَةُ أَبْوابٍ, there are two views. One of them is what was narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام): "That Hell has seven gates, layers one above the other." He placed one of his hands over the other and said: "Like this. And Allah placed the Paradises on the earth, and placed the Fires one above the other. The lowest of them is Jahannam, above it is Laza, above it is al-Hutamah, above it is Saqar, above it is al-Jahim, above it is al-Sa‘ir, and above it is al-Hawiyah." In the narration of al-Kalbi: "The lowest of them is al-Hawiyah and the highest of them is Jahannam."

In al-Khisal, from Abu ‘Abdillah, from his father, from his grandfather, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "Indeed, Paradise has eight gates: a gate through which the prophets and the truthful ones (الصديقون) enter; a gate through which the martyrs and the righteous (الصالحون) enter; and five gates through which our Shi‘ah and our lovers enter.

I shall remain standing on the Bridge (الصراط), calling out and saying: 'My Lord, save my Shi‘ah, my lovers, my helpers, and whoever took me as a guardian in the abode of the world.' Then the call will come from the depths of the Throne: 'Your prayer has been answered, and you have been granted intercession (شفاعة) for your Shi‘ah.' And every man from my Shi‘ah, and from those who took me as a guardian, helped me, and fought those who fought me by deed or word, shall intercede for seventy thousand of his neighbors and relatives.

And a gate through which the rest of the Muslims enter—those who testify that there is no god but Allah, and in whose hearts there was not an atom's weight of hatred toward us, the Ahl al-Bayt."
